data.title: London’s house price plunge is a warning sign for the whole economy
data.description:
Prices in Westminster are down by 12pc over the last year. In Kensington and Chelsea they are down by 11.2pc and by just as much in the City of London.

Over the last few weeks, it has become clear that house prices in the swankiest parts of London are in freefall. Plenty of people will be relaxed about that, and a few might even celebrate it if it means that living in the capital becomes more affordable. There is just one catch. It has the potential to blow up the whole economy – and a housing crash is the last thing Britain needs right now.

Anyone who thought that an apartment, or even better a whole house, in one of the smarter parts of London was the safest possible investment, or indeed a guarantee of lavish retirement, will have had a rude shock if they have checked Rightmove recently.

Prices are collapsing. According to the latest data from the Office for National Statistics, prices in all London’s most affluent boroughs are suffering from double-digit declines, and have now been falling for five consecutive months.

They are still expensive by any normal standards. In Kensington and Chelsea, the average price is still £1.2m, and in Westminster £872,000.

But they have suffered the biggest slump since the 2008-09 financial crash, and are now back to 2013 levels. In effect, a whole decade of gains has been wiped out, and if you were unlucky enough to have bought at anything close to the peak you will now be sitting on a big loss by now.

In fairness, across the rest of the country, there is not going to be a lot of sympathy. Prices in central London were wildly overvalued anyway. Many of the properties were owned by rich foreigners, who hardly even bothered to visit them very often. The speculators can afford the losses. It might even make it easier for first-time buyers to find somewhere to live. If anything, we should be celebrating housing becoming a little more affordable. It will make life easier for everyone.

Perhaps. The trouble is, it could also turn into a huge problem for the wider British economy. There are four big issues.

To start with, it is the most direct confirmation we have that the wealthy are fleeing London. It is the only explanation for the sudden falls: people want to leave and they don’t mind taking a loss on the way out. They might not be going to Dubai any more, but they are going somewhere else instead. Closing the non-dom scheme, raising capital gains taxes and the highest inheritance tax in the developed world have driven them out of the capital.

The class warriors in Labour and the Green Party might welcome that. Bashing the rich is their favourite occupation. But as you lose the wealthiest people, you also lose all the jobs they create and the investment they bring into the country as well. It is hardly good for the economy.

Next, it is going to turn into a huge drain on the Treasury. All those Westminster and Chelsea apartments were paying 12pc in stamp duty and often more if the owners were foreign or if it was a second home. Stamp duty brings in £15bn a year, and on some estimates more than half of that came from the top band on properties above £1.5m.

At a rough estimate, a collapse in the London market would cost Rachel Reeves between £6bn and £7bn a year, and since this Government refuses to control spending, that money would have to be found from higher borrowing or higher taxes on the rest of us. As for the planned mansion tax on properties worth more than £2m, perhaps we should scrap it now. At this rate, there won’t be enough left to make it worth the hassle.

Thirdly, it will trap lots of people in negative equity. Anyone who bought at any time over the last decade, especially if they took out a big mortgage, as most of them probably did, will now be stuck with a property that is worth less than the debt secured on it.

They won’t be able to move even if they can sell their home, because they will have no way of repaying the loan. If they lose their job and can’t keep up repayments, the mortgage lender will have to take the hit. Just as seriously, through the Help To Buy scheme, the Government may well face heavy losses on its own loans, and that will leave the public finances in even worse shape than they already are.

Finally, price drops in central London are often the first signal of wider falls across the country. House prices nationally have held up remarkably well so far despite the steep rise in interest rates, all the extra taxes, and a stagnating economy. But who says that is going to last?

Anyone who is quietly pleased about the losses suffered on a swanky apartment in Chelsea won’t be quite so happy when very ordinary homes in Bristol or Newcastle are witnessing double-digit price declines. A general house price crash always starts somewhere. You have to be very optimistic to think that falling prices in central London won’t be rippling out to the rest of the country by the end of the year.

In reality, a property crash is the last thing the British economy needs right now. The economy is already close to a recession. Inflation is poised to rise following the surge in oil prices in the wake of the war in Iran leaving the Bank of England with no room to cut interest rates.

The Chancellor is spending so wildly that taxes will inevitably have to rise again in the autumn. There are few jobs being created, and investment is evaporating. It is a dismal outlook.

If house prices start to slide, it would crush consumer spending, and pose a threat to the financial system as well. It hasn’t happened yet – but unfortunately the signals from Westminster and Kensington suggest that it could be on the way.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Penalise councils for planning delays, Lidl urges Labour
data.description:
Lidl has urged ministers to penalise councils over planning hold-ups as its expansion drive is blown off course by local authority delays.

The German discount chain claimed the UK’s planning system remained “stuck in the slow lane” despite pledges by Sir Keir Starmer to “get Britain building”.

Writing in The Telegraph, Richard Taylor, Lidl’s UK chief real estate officer, criticised a “lack of consistency” in decision-making from planning officers and public bodies such as the Environment Agency and Natural England.

Lidl is planning to open 50 new supermarkets during the next year as it battles to overtake Morrisons and become Britain’s fifth-biggest grocer. It is hunting for sites in Garthdee in Aberdeen, Aldgate in London and Windsor, Berkshire.

The German discounter has more than 1,010 supermarkets in the UK, up from 880 in August 2021. However, Lidl’s pace of expansion has been slower than it initially planned. In 2021, it set out a target to have 1,100 stores by the end of 2025.

Mr Taylor said Lidl “could have invested in far more communities by now” if it had not faced planning hold-ups.

He added: “We need a system that rewards efficiency and penalises stagnation.

“By introducing clear, mandatory milestones – backed by meaningful incentives for local authorities that meet statutory timelines – the Government can transform the planning system from a bottleneck into an engine for growth.”

It follows repeated pledges by Labour to push through a sweeping shake-up of the planning system as part of its “build, baby, build” drive.

Last November, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, unveiled £48m of funding to “boost capacity” in the planning system, including recruiting an extra 350 officers across Britain.

However, Mr Taylor said this was failing to shift the dial for companies going through the planning process.

He said: “The reality is, we are yet to see the benefit of this.

“Funding alone isn’t enough, and we, instead, need planning officers to have the resources and expertise to process applications efficiently.”

He claimed some applications were still taking more than two years for a decision, despite statutory targets for authorities to get this down to 13 weeks from the application being validated.

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “Our Planning and Infrastructure Act is now law to go even further to speed up local decisions and accelerate building at scale.”

Our planning system is in desperate need of a rebuild

by Richard Taylor

I recently celebrated 20 years of working in property at Lidl GB. Two decades spent helping what was once the fresh-faced upstart become Britain’s fastest-growing bricks-and-mortar supermarket – a title we’ve held for nearly three years now.

But those same two decades have also been spent navigating a planning system which is in desperate need of a rebuild.

Commercial development is a force for good. It provides vital goods and services to people across the country.

It creates jobs, promotes economic growth and improves living standards. In our case, it enables us to open more stores and give more communities across the country access to healthy and affordable food.

Since the first Lidl GB store opened in 1994, we have grown to more than 1,000 stores, with more than 50 new stores planned for the next 12 months alone.

Our ambition is limitless, but to keep investing, we need a planning system that encourages quality development rather than frustrating it. We have the ambition, but the planning system is stuck in the slow lane.

For too long, the planning system has hindered rather than enabled progress. While the statutory target is 13 weeks from validation to decision, some of our applications now take two years or more.

That is two years of lost high-paying jobs, access to affordable food, and lost donations and community grants for local food banks or community groups. Had these delays not existed, we could have invested in far more communities by now.

I am encouraged by the Government’s efforts to reform the planning system and welcome the Chancellor’s announcement in last year’s Budget to make more funding available for new planning officers.

But the reality is, we have yet to see the benefit of this. Funding alone isn’t enough, and we instead need planning officers to have the resources and expertise to process applications efficiently.

The Housing Secretary’s “default yes” policy on certain planning applications is also a positive step forward, but reforms must be joined-up.

Local plans should ensure commercial and residential developments are complementary, rather than conflicting. They should ensure that businesses are at the heart of local communities and residents can access the services they need in their locality.

This is why we champion innovative approaches such as the wide-ranging development of mixed-use schemes that can help local communities achieve this balance.

Achieving an effective and faster planning system will be no mean feat. It will take collaboration across the public and private sectors. But we’ve seen when it works, the results show.

Take our upcoming site in Wolverhampton, for example, which took less than 12 weeks to go from submission to determination.

Because of that efficiency, we’re on track to open the store a full year earlier than anticipated. That’s an extra year of wages for a new store team and an extra year of affordable food to local households.

During my career, I have watched planning timelines slip from weeks to years. This lack of consistency across local planning authorities and statutory consultees does more than just delay a store opening.

It breaks the confidence of the entire supply chain. From the contractors waiting to break ground to the local suppliers and community, everyone is left in a state of expensive limbo.

To break this cycle, the Government must move beyond policy and focus on accountability. We need a system that rewards efficiency and penalises stagnation.

By introducing clear, mandatory milestones – backed by meaningful incentives for local authorities that meet statutory timelines – the Government can transform the planning system from a bottleneck into an engine for growth.

The message from us is clear: the ambition is there, the capital is ready, and the community demand is proven. 

If the Government can bridge the gap between policy intent and local execution, they will do more than just build supermarkets – they will unlock the investment required to boost the British economy.

Richard Taylor is Lidl’s UK chief real estate officer

data.title: South East Water boss waives bonus after ‘getting it wrong’ on supply crisis
data.description:
The boss of South East Water has admitted that he “got it wrong” during outages in Tunbridge Wells as he gave up his bonus over the disruption.

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the embattled utility company, said he had made mistakes during the incident, which left thousands of homes without water for weeks. It resulted in schools in Kent closing and elderly residents queuing for two hours for bottled water.

Mr Hinton said he had “got it wrong” over how he dealt with the public. Chris Train, the company’s chairman, confirmed that Mr Hinton would not be taking his £115,000 bonus for this year.

He received a £400,000 basic salary and a £115,000 bonus last year, as well as being paid a further £50,000 for working overtime.

The chief executive was notably absent during the crisis, which began in November 2025. About 24,000 homes and businesses across the South East lost drinking water for two weeks.

Mr Hinton refused to speak to the media during the outages and attacked Mike Martin, a local MP, for having “politicised the issue”.

‘We accept that we failed’

However, appearing before MPs on the Environment Committee on Tuesday, Mr Hinton admitted the company’s response had been “disappointing” and said mistakes were made in how quickly he communicated with the public.

He said: “That is very much a lesson that we have learnt for the playbook of how we handle future events.”

Mr Hinton said South East Water “should have reacted on those earlier warnings quicker, and our problem-solving around it when we got into that situation was too slow and unstructured”.

Mr Train, the non-executive chairman of the utility company, said: “We accept that we failed in our primary duty and we could have done better.”

The admission follows an earlier hearing when Mr Hinton had sought to defend himself before the Environment Committee. During the first appearance, he told MPs that the outage, which was caused by the failure of a water treatment plant, was not foreseeable.

He rated himself eight out of 10 for his response, claiming that “lifestyle changes”, including more people working from home, were partly to blame for the incident.

‘Cat with nine lives’

The earlier hearing came the same day as fresh disruption hit the Tunbridge Wells area. On Jan 7, the taps ran dry again in Tunbridge Wells because of thawing pipes cracking. It affected 25,000 homes in the area.

On Tuesday, Mr Train would not be drawn on whether there would be any failure sufficient that he would ask Mr Hinton to step down.

Henry Tufnell, an MP who sits on the Environment Committee, told The Telegraph: “Hinton’s capacity to survive is remarkable. He’s like a cat with nine lives.”

Mr Tufnell added: “We used to pride ourselves on being able to turn on the tap [to have] high-quality drinking water. To have lost that and to have lost that trust and confidence is just shocking and quite damning.”

data.title: I visited Britain’s most battered train bridge. No wonder the railways are in chaos
data.description:
For people living near Watling Street in Hinckley, it’s not unusual to be woken in the night by a thunderous bang as another lorry comes to grief at the local railway bridge.

This small Leicestershire town is home to Britain’s most battered bridge. Surrounded by depots of enormous vehicles, it was struck at least 22 times by lorries and buses throughout 2024 and 2025.

Across the country, a railway bridge is hit by a road vehicle every five hours, with about 1,700 strikes on bridges recorded by Network Rail between April 2024 and March 2025.

The result was 120 days of delays to train journeys last year and a £23m bill for taxpayers, with each strike costing about £13,000.

Some 43 per cent of lorry drivers admit to not measuring their vehicle before setting off and 52 per cent do not take low bridges into account.

Outside Hinckley on the A5, heavy goods vehicles of all types, from concrete mixers to delivery vans, push their luck at speed, zooming eye-wateringly close to the underbelly of the bridge that bears the scars of past collisions.

Each time one crunches underneath the steelwork, mayhem ensues, with long traffic jams, train delays and debris littering the road, according to residents.

“It’s a nightmare,” one neighbour says. “It’s frightening, like a bang or an explosion. It happens all the time, at least once a week.”

The bridge’s luminous yellow-striped warning signs are visibly scarred by scrapes, while the supporting walls are missing large chunks of brick.

In the lay-by, splinters and vehicle parts lie scattered through the undergrowth and residents of a nearby caravan park report tables, chairs and even mattresses flying out of lorries.

A mother with two young daughters sighs as she recalls how the bangs shake the caravan almost weekly.

Chantelle Callton, 35, tells The Telegraph: “Oh my God, it shakes. It’s like a bomb’s gone off. Everybody speeds down here, so it’s very dangerous.”

The bridge is so notorious that she was warned about it more than six years ago before she moved there.

“If we go a week when it’s not been hit, we’ll say: ‘We’re due one soon,’” she adds.

Dianna Smith, 60, who runs the caravan site with her husband, says her son and his friend had to help a trapped driver out of a lorry in the night. “It’s a ticking time-bomb until something bad happens,” she adds.

“They get that much speed up and don’t realise they’re too high. One lorry’s top was pulled back like a tin opener. They have to stop the trains and it messes everything up.”

Betty Watton, 44, sitting outside with her two chihuahuas, lives closest to the bridge and says drivers often flee the scene.

She says: “One lorry tore the whole top off and just left it in the road, rolled up like a big piece of scrap. One time paint tins spilt and paint went everywhere, but it’s mainly parcels.”

At a nearby depot filled with HGVs and lorries, workers clad in orange hi-vis laugh knowingly at the mention of the bridge.

They refuse to speak, but one jokes to another: “How many times have you hit it?”

Vehicles over a certain height at the Greenway Environmental depot have to take lengthy detours to avoid the bridge, including driving on to the motorway, the assistant manager says.

Another worker suggests it might not be the most strategic location for a lorry depot.

“Earlier this year, one was full of papers, cardboard and packaging. When that got ripped apart, it peeled the lid back and paper and card flew everywhere, blocking the street off for quite a while,” he says.

“There are a lot of industrial sites and major warehouses here. The kind of places that have drivers that are coming here for the very first time. They follow their sat nav, but still...

“There are a lot of light-up signs the whole way saying: ‘High vehicle. Turn back.’ But it’s become quite an issue that lorry drivers use their phones while driving.”

Mountpark, which is funding the project and working with National Highways, has planned to lower the road under the bridge since 2023, but critics of the scheme fear it would be complex and lead to flooding.

Karen, the landlady of a nearby pub, says road works will only add to the traffic jams blighting her business.

With the construction of a new supermarket depot around the bridge, she fears that the problem will not be going away anytime soon.

data.title: Tories promise to abolish 24-hour bus lanes
data.description:
The Conservatives have pledged to abolish 24-hour bus lanes to ease traffic congestion.

The party is launching their six-point plan for motorists before the local elections on May 7 with a promise of “bus lanes that make sense”.

Kemi Badenoch, the Tory leader, said they would restrict the operating hours of bus lanes to allow cars to use them more frequently.

Mrs Badenoch said Labour wanted “to make drivers’ lives as difficult as possible”. In recent years, the number of bus lanes operating 24 hours a day, seven days a week, has soared in cities across the Midlands and Merseyside.

In London, more than 50 miles of bus lanes became a 24-hour operation in 2021 under Heidi Alexander, the former deputy mayor for transport and now the transport secretary.

Under Tory plans, all 24/7 bus lanes will be scrapped unless a government minister provides specific written permission for one to remain open.

Conservative sources pointed to a Tory council in West Northamptonshire scrapping a 24-hour bus lane, making it only operational between 7.30am and 9.30am.

Manchester city council issued the highest number of fines for bus lane violations, at more than 500,000 between 2023 and 2025.

According to data from used-car firm Cinch, Bristol city council came second at 344,000 fines, followed by Sheffield city council at 275,360.

The implementation and operation of bus lanes is down to individual councils to decide what is best for their area.

Government guidance says bus lanes “should be provided only when they are needed” and operate “only when buses are running” or when traffic is sufficiently busy to cause delays.

It adds that local authorities should make sure that if a bus lane does operate at quieter times of the day, there is a strong case for it and the effect on other users is “mitigated”.

The Tories’ six-point plan for motorists also includes a promise to scrap so-called “blanket” 20mph speed limits across the country.

Tory councils will review all 20mph measures, with 30mph limits restored on roads where 20mph limits are widely ignored.

The Conservatives have also promised to scrap the rise in fuel duty planned by the Chancellor for September, and unroll a “national pothole patrol” to fix Britain’s roads.

The party also said it would recruit more driving test examiners and increase training capacity to reduce the backlog, which has risen by more than three weeks under Labour.

A Labour spokesman said: “Nobody should let the Tories behind the wheel again. After 14 years in charge, they left our roads riddled with potholes and the driving test system in chaos, with waiting times spiralling on their watch.”

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: BP hit by investor rebellion after net zero retreat
data.description:
BP has been hit by a major investor rebellion after watering down its net zero plans to instead focus on oil and gas.

Almost a fifth of investors voted against the re-election of Albert Manifold, the company’s new chairman, at the business’s annual general meeting (AGM) on Thursday.

Mr Manifold, who became chairman in September, was re-elected with just 81.77pc of voting shareholders’ backing. Most executives at major public companies are elected with the backing of more than 95pc of investors.

Legal & General, one of Europe’s biggest asset managers, had said before the AGM that it would vote against Mr Manifold’s re-election, while Glass Lewis, a proxy adviser, recommended investors do the same.

Shareholders also rejected BP’s attempts to move to online AGMs and scrap the publication of company-specific climate reports.

Investors have been angered by the company’s decision to retreat from net zero plans drawn up in 2020, water down climate reporting and refocus wholeheartedly on oil and gas.

A former construction materials chief, one of Mr Manifold’s first moves was to appoint Meg O’Neill, a seasoned oil executive, to lead the company.

He also refused to bring a shareholder climate resolution to a vote and provoked controversy by planning to end BP’s climate impact reporting commitments.

Thursday’s stormy meeting saw a raft of shareholder challenges to executives over their plans, with one persistent questioner finally removed by BP’s security guards.

A spokesman for Rathbones said the wealth manager had bought 43 million shares in BP based on the company’s net zero commitments and demanded: “safeguards to provide assurance to shareholders that their rights ... will be protected.”

Addressing shareholders at the gathering in Sunbury-on-Thames, Surrey, Mr Manifold said: “BP’s performance in recent years has not been where you – or we – want it to be. The job is to ensure BP delivers the sustainable, long-term returns you expect from us.

“We need to be a simpler, stronger, more valuable company. In service of that, we have made decisive changes to BP’s strategy and to the leadership.”

Ms O’Neill, who joined BP this month from Australian oil firm Woodside Energy, pledged more change in pursuit of bigger profits.

She said: “BP is a great company. It can be even greater. Now, we need to accelerate the progress we have made so far. We need to strengthen the balance sheet and be rigorous in our spending and investment. Our next chapter will be as a simpler, stronger, more valuable company.”

Ms O’Neill was re-elected with far more support, winning the backing of 96.76pc of shareholders who voted.

Ms O’Neill is the first woman to take on the top job at the 117-year-old company. However, she is also BP’s third chief executive in under five years.

Gavin Palmer, who described himself as a BP investor and investment analyst, was removed from the meeting after criticising the company, demanding: “How did you get through so many chief executives?”

A key AGM battle was over the board’s proposal to repeal its 2019 pledge to report detailed climate-related information, including its management of planet-heating emissions, low-carbon investments, public policy positions and how executive pay is linked to progress.

The company wanted to ditch these “legacy” obligations in favour of wider industry reporting guidelines. Campaigners argued this would let BP exclude much of the detail of its environmental impacts.

Investors rejected BP’s attempt to scrap the reporting requirements.

A separate but related battle was over the BP board’s rejection of a shareholder resolution from the Dutch activist group Follow This. It wanted BP to publish plans for how it would maintain shareholder value as fossil-fuel demand falls with the transition to clean energy.

A similar resolution has been accepted at Shell and will go to a shareholder vote at its meeting next month.

Mark van Baal, the chief executive of Follow This, accused Mr Manifold of “deepening BP’s governance crisis”.

Mr Manifold claimed the proposal was legally invalid. BP has said the board had decided, having taken legal advice, that the proposed resolution “did not conform to legal requirements” and would be ineffective if it were to pass.

data.title: Bank of England urges lenders to strengthen defences against AI
data.description:
The Bank of England has told City firms to bolster their cyber defences against the threat of new artificial intelligence-powered hacking bots.

Banks, insurers and payments firms were summoned to a meeting on Wednesday to discuss the cybersecurity risks posed by “frontier AI models”, which include Anthropic’s new Mythos bot that was deemed too dangerous to release to the public.

At the meeting, which was held by the Bank of England’s Cross Market Operational Resilience Group (CMORG), City firms were urged to strengthen their cybersecurity.

This could be done by using AI to fix vulnerabilities and automate responses to any threats, CMORG said, adding that it planned to hold another meeting in May with a broader range of companies.

CMORG was set up in 2019 to deal with systemic threats faced by Britain’s finance industry.

It comes after revelations about Anthropic’s new Mythos model prompted panic among cybersecurity experts, who raised concerns that the AI tool could wreak havoc if it ends up in the wrong hands.

Anthropic unveiled the Mythos model earlier this month but has held back from releasing it publicly.

The AI developer has warned Mythos’s hacking abilities “surpass all but the most skilled humans at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities”, and said the model threatens to “reshape cybersecurity”.

The warning led to Wall Street banks being summoned to a crisis meeting this month by Scott Bessent, the US treasury secretary, and Jerome Powell, the chairman of the Federal Reserve.

MI5 is also racing to bolster the defences of Britain’s critical infrastructure, urging water, energy and communications companies to be aware of Mythos’s capabilities.

An evaluation of Mythos by the Artificial Intelligence Security Institute, a UK-funded research lab, found that the model was able to autonomously carry out advanced cyberattacks that would take a human hacker days.

In its report, the research lab added that future AI models “will be more capable still”, as it urged companies to boost their cyber defences.

Banks and technology companies including Amazon, Apple and JP Morgan have been given access to Anthropic’s powerful AI tool to let them stress-test their cyber defences, through a scheme called Project Glasswing.

However, on Tuesday, Anthropic revealed it was investigating claims that some unauthorised people had gained access to the Mythos tool through the scheme.

It said it did not have evidence the tool had been accessed beyond the “vendor environment”.

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: The collapse of Starmer’s housebuilding revolution
data.description:
For Weston Homes, a mid-sized property developer in London’s commuter belt, Sir Keir Starmer’s election victory was supposed to be a godsend.

The Labour leader had promised to “bulldoze” England’s planning system and unlock a housebuilding revolution, staking his reputation on building an unprecedented 1.5 million homes by 2030.

But almost two years on, something has gone badly wrong. Before Sir Keir was elected, Weston Homes was building 1,000 new homes per year. This has now plunged to 600.

“Our output was probably last that low at the back end of the global financial crisis,” said Bob Weston, the company’s chairman.

“The UK might not be in a technical economic recession, but we are, without question, in a housing recession.

“Everybody is scaling back. Construction industry jobs are falling like flies. Pretty much every construction site in the UK is on go-slow.”

Weston says the outlook for housebuilders is the worst he has seen in his 50-year career.

At a national level, the data tells an alarming tale.

Despite Labour’s promises, the number of new homes built last year fell to a decade-low.

Even more worryingly, the number of homes securing planning consent tumbled by 27pc to levels last seen in the early 2010s.

Campaign group Britain Remade estimates that, based on the current pipeline and average build times, England is on track to deliver just 706,000 new homes over this parliament.

This represents just 47pc of Labour’s target and 5pc less than the number of new homes delivered under the previous government.

In London, analysts expect the lowest annual building rates in the post-Second World War era.

A range of problems is underlying this latest crunch, including time.

Although Labour has made a series of structural planning reforms widely commended by industry, including scope to unlock swathes of land, it will be years before the benefits are realised.

It is also the sad reality that planning was only ever part of the problem.

Elsewhere, developers are burdened by red tape and too many people still cannot afford to buy new-build homes.

The war in Iran has also now unleashed more turmoil, as soaring energy prices and elevated borrowing costs are bound to increase construction costs and dent demand.

Ultimately, it appears Sir Keir’s housebuilding pledge is dead in the water.

Housing boom on paper only

To its credit, Labour acted swiftly to tackle the planning system.

Within days of the election in July 2024, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, announced that mandatory housing targets would be reimposed on local authorities.

By the end of the month, Labour had published a draft national planning policy framework (NPPF) that increased the number of homes councils were expected to approve.

It also created a “grey belt” designation to unlock development on protected green belt land. Data company LandTech estimated that this could open up space for up to four million new homes.

A new draft NPPF published last December also outlined plans to encourage development in the “train belt”.

The idea being that if developers submit plans to build projects of a certain density within 800 metres of major railway stations, they should be approved by default. Lichfields planning consultants expect that this will unlock land to develop 633,000 homes.

This radical policy shift has been effective.

Local authorities are allocating sites for housing quicker, while also prompting conversations with housebuilders about increasing development, says Mark Adams, the executive chairman of Pennyfarthing, a housebuilder in the South of England.

Because planning applications are now much more likely to get waved through, Britain’s builders are also submitting more of them.

A senior source at a major housebuilder says their firm has doubled planning applications made on sites in their long-term land portfolio as a result of these reforms.

“Two years ago, we were progressing with planning applications for about 33pc of our portfolio,” they said. “Now it’s about 66pc. So that’s a 100pc increase.

“In the quarter of a century I’ve been doing this, there’s been nothing like as positive a planning system as we’ve got now.”

But this does not mean more homes are actually getting built, because council planners remain woefully under-resourced. That is alongside frequent opposition from local Nimbys (not in my back yard).

This has sparked a planning backlog as applications for major sites are taking two or three years to be approved.

The number of homes gaining full planning permission fell in every single quarter last year, according to Savills.

Across 2025, just 180,000 properties were approved for development. Not only was this the lowest level in more than 20 years, but it was also down by a quarter from the year before Labour won the election.

In London, the number of homes getting planning consent more than halved year on year.

Stuck in the doldrums

According to Simon Coop, of Lichfields planning consultants, there has been no real change in the way that local authorities approach planning decisions.

“Nearly two years into the parliamentary term, you’re getting the sense that nothing’s changing,” he says.

Ironically, the new surge in planning applications may have made things worse, says Greg Hill, of The Hill Group, a housebuilder in London and the South.

“The system remains very challenging, very slow,” he says. “Because there are more applications in the system, it puts more pressure on the system. So there’s planning activity but not housing delivery activity.”

In 2024, Labour announced it would employ 300 new planning officers, but this has so far made little difference.

This week, the British Property Federation (BPF) warned that it will take an additional 3,000 officers to give local authorities the manpower needed to manage their workloads.

Low levels of planning approvals mean just 3.4pc of the population live in local authorities which are on track to meet their new housing targets, according to Britain Remade.

Savills’ analysis shows that in London, which has a housing target of 88,000 per year, the number of homes that secured planning consent last year was little more than 20,000.

The national pipeline is also dismal. In 2025, just 202,800 new homes were completed, the lowest total in any calendar year since 2015, including the pandemic.

This was down 3pc year on year, 18pc less than in 2019 and nearly a third short of the Government’s annual target of building 300,000 homes per year.

Starts on new homes has climbed since Labour took office, but they are still in the doldrums.

Office for National Statistics (ONS) data show that work started on 118,000 new homes in England in the 12 months to September.

Red tape burden

This was up by 30pc compared to the 12 months before the 2024 election. But excluding that period, it was the lowest 12-month total since 2013.

James Moody, at London property developer Seven Capital, says: “Their manifesto pledge has failed. That is the honest answer.”

Even Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, acknowledged the crisis last month by warning that a lack of new homes meant young people were living with their parents into their 40s.

“It’s not acceptable,” he said. “The opportunities that were open to my generation, when we were younger, are closed off to them.”

One key factor Sir Keir seems to have overlooked is that planning is only part of the problem.

While Labour has done a good job of reducing planning hurdles, it has not addressed the regulatory burdens on construction, which are growing, says Sam Dumitriu, at Britain Remade, an economic think tank.

For decades, housebuilders have had to make large financial contributions to the local areas where they are building.

From 2010, councils have been able to charge developers for new infrastructure under the Community Infrastructure Levy (CIL).

This came on top of pre-existing requirements for housebuilders to make contributions to affordable housing under Section 106 of the Town and Country Planning Act.

When Sir Sadiq Khan was elected Mayor of London in 2016, he set a requirement that 35pc of new housing developments should be “affordable” homes, which are sold at below market rates.

The 2017 Grenfell fire, which killed 72 people, triggered a new wave of building safety regulations.

From April 2022, large builders have had to pay a Residential Property Developer Tax (RPDT) to fund the country’s cladding remediation bill.

In the same year, requirements were introduced for the new Building Safety Regulator to approve plans for high-rise developments.

This process takes an average of nine months to give sign-off and has so far seen 69pc of pre-construction applications get rejected, according to Britain Remade.

From October 2026, developers will also have to pay the building safety levy, another tax to pay for cladding remediation.

At the same time, environmental rules have tightened dramatically.

Self-storage ‘more valuable’

In 2019, the Government introduced “nutrient neutrality” rules that mean housing developments must not increase soil pollutants in protected areas.

The Future Homes Standard, which will come into effect in March 2027, will also require new homes to be fitted with low-carbon forms of heating.

“There isn’t anyone who does what we do who hasn’t got a long list of examples of sites where they’re held up due to red tape, bureaucracy, regulations and other items,” says Hill. “We have a litany of examples.”

Mandatory timelines for decision-making are rarely adhered to, he adds. “We’re perpetually in delay on everything we do.”

A new report by the Home Builders Federation (HBF) being published next week is expected to show that in the last five years, the cost of building a new home has increased by more than £70,000 because of new taxes, regulations and build cost inflation.

Zoopla estimated last year that half of the country was no longer viable to develop.

At Seven Capital’s 100 Kensington development, a 462-home project under construction in west London, the company is paying nearly £70m in developer taxes and community contributions at the site, says Moody.

This is about a third of the project’s total build costs, which are roughly double what they would have been a decade ago, he says.

“The planning tax is about £60m more than we will make, and that is before I pay tax on any profits,” says Moody.

“I’ve been doing this for 30 years and I’ve been through a number of recessions. I think this is the most difficult set of circumstances I’ve ever operated in. Because we live in an environment full of regulators, not facilitators.”

Seven Capital has another 178-home development in Islington, which has been in the planning process since 2021.

Since Seven Capital logged the planning application, inflation and new regulations have increased the cost of building the site by about 30pc, says Moody. Sale prices, however, have not moved.

“It is probably more valuable as a self-storage site than a housing site.”

Jon Stoddart, co-head of planning at CBRE, says: “It’s just a very heavily taxed industry. Whilst the Government has had some really good policy initiatives, there still seems to be a fundamental issue around the viability of development.”

London falling

These issues are most acute in London – partly because the capital has a higher density of tall buildings subject to new building safety regulations, but also because there are not enough buyers who can afford to pay for what the builders are selling.

The average home in the capital costs 10.6 times a typical person’s earnings. Technically, this ratio has eased from its 2021 peak of 12.9, but because interest rates have climbed since Covid, mortgage costs are actually far higher.

Based on current rates, the monthly mortgage bill for a buyer purchasing an average London home with a 10pc deposit is now £3,000, up from £2,000 in 2021. Zoopla calculated last year that a buyer needs to earn more than £100,000 per year to afford to buy a property in the capital.

“The biggest thing for housebuilding at the moment, aside from the planning, is demand,” a source at a major housebuilder says. “People need to be able to afford to buy. That side of the coin has been neglected.”

Squeezed affordability matters even more for developers now because of the loss of the Help to Buy equity loan scheme, which ran from 2013 until 2023.

Nationally, this supported around 40,000 new build sales per year, by providing first-time buyers purchasing new homes with 20pc government-backed equity loans that were interest-free for the first five years.

“First-time buyers are in the worst situation they’ve been in for decades,” says Weston.

Before the pandemic, first-time buyers made up 70pc of Weston Homes’ buyers. Now, they purchase only 30pc of the homes that Weston sells.

The Help-to-Buy crutch was removed at the same time that the Government was pushing a buy-to-let tax crackdown, which has eroded the business case for private rental investment in the capital.

Changes to anti-money laundering rules, falling prices and a new stamp duty surcharge for overseas buyers in 2021 have also hit demand among foreign investors.

New-build developers were heavily reliant on these two buyer groups to purchase new homes off plan, which was a vital source of cash flow during development, says James Barton, joint head of London Land Agency at Knight Frank. Now these buyers have dried up.

“Developers can deal with two or three headwinds, but at the moment they have a perfect storm of eight or nine issues that they can’t control,” says Barton.

London is now at the epicentre of Britain’s housebuilding drought. Private housing starts in London fell by half in 2025 to just 5,500, according to data company Molior. This was 83pc less than in 2015.

Based on the capital’s current pipeline, Molior expects just 14,053 homes will be completed across 2027 and 2028, just 8pc of the Government’s two-year London housing target of 176,000.

This would be the first time since 1946 that London has completed no more than 10,000 homes a year.

And what is in the pipeline is being put on hold. There are currently 893 planning permissions for unbuilt schemes in London, according to Molior. In the last 12 months, construction started on just 73 of these.

“More than 90pc of permissions just aren’t going anywhere,” says Tim Craine, Molior’s founder.

Ben Hopkinson, at the Centre for Policy Studies, says: “It’s very, very bad. London’s facing its strongest headwinds for housebuilding since the Second World War.

“Starts are down that much that, if something drastic doesn’t happen, we’re set for record low levels of housebuilding.”

In October, Sir Sadiq and Reed announced a package of emergency measures to boost London housebuilding, which included reducing the affordable homes requirement from 35pc to 20pc. However, analysts warn it is not enough to shift the dial.

At the start of April, London-focused housebuilder Berkeley Group said it was halting land purchases because of “unprecedented” increases in costs and regulation.

“We have ended up in a situation where the place where we need to build homes the most has become the place where it’s hardest to build homes. It’s a real disaster,” says Dumitriu.

Impact of war in Iran

Now, the war in Iran has made a bad situation even worse.

“Seven weeks ago, things were genuinely improving,” says an industry source. “Mortgage affordability was improving and you were seeing an improvement in sales rates. Then Iran happened.”

Iran’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has sent oil prices soaring past $100 per barrel, reviving inflation fears and driving up government borrowing costs. Mortgage rates have jumped as traders price in the prospect of Bank of England interest rate rises.

The average rate on a two-year fixed-rate mortgage has climbed from 4.83pc at the start of March to 5.89pc, according to Moneyfacts. For a buyer taking out a typical £200,000 home loan, this has added £3,024 in interest payments to the cost of the loan over two years.

The blow to buyer demand is significant. Adams, of Pennyfarthing, says the number of buyers visiting the company’s new-build sites has plunged by 50pc since the war began.

“There’s this lack of confidence because of what’s going on in the UK and the wider world,” says Adams. “People can see where mortgage rates are going and they are looking over their shoulder at energy prices and the general cost of living.”

Pennyfarthing has contractors lined up at a site in the South of England, which has planning permission for 340 homes.

However, it has now put the development on hold, in large part resulting from fears over falling demand. “We’ve had it on a rolling review for the last four weeks,” says Adams.

The other big looming threat is rising construction costs, a source at a housebuilder warns: “The longer the war goes on, the worse the impact gets.”

UK construction costs in February were already 42pc higher than in January 2020, after the pandemic supply chain crisis and inflation wave.

Soon, higher oil prices will flow through to higher costs of bricks, steel, concrete and other energy-intensive construction products.

Housebuilders face a “perfect storm” in the second half of this year as they brace for a double whammy of elevated costs and lower demand, warns Noble Francis, chief economist at the Construction Products Association (CPA).

Consequently, the listed UK housebuilder stocks have tanked. Barratt Redrow, Berkeley Group, Vistry and Taylor Wimpey have dropped by 32pc, 14pc, 47pc and 21pc respectively so far this year.

Whitehall sources have also been warning that the war jeopardises the Government’s target to build 1.5 million homes.

For Sir Keir, failing to deliver on housebuilding means not only an acute shortage of homes but also the death of Labour’s promise to secure the highest sustained growth rate in the G7.

New housing creates growth partly through construction sector jobs and profits, but also by reducing rents and enabling the workforce to be able to move to where the best jobs are, which boosts productivity.

“If Labour don’t have that growth story from housing, then where is the growth going to come from?” says Dumitriu.

“A failure to get a grip on this housing issue by the end of this parliament means they’re going to be in real trouble.”

A spokesman for the Housing Department said: “We will build the homes this country needs.

“We’ve overhauled the planning system and invested a record £39bn in social and affordable housing to help developers get spades in the ground and weather any geopolitical pressure.

“This is alongside emergency measures in London to unblock dozens of stalled sites and build thousands more homes.”

data.title: Drivers ‘owed hundreds of pounds more’ in motor finance scandal
data.description:
Drivers who were mis-sold car finance should be getting hundreds of pounds more in compensation, a consumer group has claimed, as it prepares to take the City watchdog to court because of its £9.1bn redress scheme.

Consumer Voice accused the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) of siding with lenders in how its compensation scheme was designed for millions of drivers who were mis-sold car finance.

The group has notified the City watchdog that it is planning to launch a legal challenge because of the programme, which will cover some 12.1 million car loan deals.

Compensation is being made to drivers who were sold car finance agreements in which dealers received a fee from lenders based on the interest rate charged on the loans.

The FCA, which banned these agreements in 2021, argued that these fees were rarely disclosed to customers and provided an incentive for dealers to charge higher interest rates than necessary.

The watchdog laid out details of its compensation scheme last month, saying millions of drivers who had signed up for these deals as long ago as 2007 would receive £830 each.

However, Alex Neill, co-founder of Consumer Voice, said: “The FCA has designed a scheme that leaves ordinary motorists hundreds of pounds per claim out of pocket. That cannot be left unchallenged.

“Consumers have been let down by the lenders who mis-sold them car finance. They should not be let down again by the regulator that is meant to protect them. The FCA must fix the scheme and deliver the fair redress that millions of UK motorists are owed.”

Consumer Voice is understood to believe that drivers are missing out on between £200 and £300 per claim. However, this could rise to between £800 and £900 in some cases.

It would mark the first time a consumer-focused group has challenged the regulator because of a compensation scheme in UK courts. It would also be the first legal challenge to the motor finance scheme.

If successful, the legal action could force lenders to carve out billions of pounds more for compensation to drivers.

It comes after years when the motor finance scandal has weighed on lenders, hitting the share price of several Britain’s biggest banks and forcing them to book billions of pounds worth of provisions.

FCA ‘underestimates the true harm suffered’

Consumer Voice helps law firms represent claimants in group actions to promote their claims. The group has partnered with law firm Courmacs Legal to file the challenge.

Courmacs Legal is separately filing a legal challenge against Lloyds Banking Group on behalf of customers, seeking damages for historic car-leasing agreements. Lloyds has declined to comment on the action.

Consumer Voice said it believed that the FCA had misapplied an earlier Supreme Court ruling on car finance, which it claimed meant that the vast majority of complaints would not receive full redress.

The group said: “Most consumers will receive redress based on a complex ‘hybrid’ redress calculation that is fundamentally flawed and significantly underestimates the true harm suffered.”

An FCA spokesman said: “Our scheme is the quickest, fairest way to compensate consumers. It seems contradictory that organisations claiming to represent consumers would seek to delay payouts for millions of people.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: London is becoming a Potemkin city
data.description:
What is happening to our cities? Physically, they are being hollowed out, as old buildings are eviscerated: their hinderparts completely destroyed and replaced, leaving only a thin veneer of original façade to the street. What a metaphor for a society that, in the political and money-making departments, seems devoid of principle.

Seeming survivals of old London, Manchester or Glasgow are often in reality no such thing. A fig leaf covers an obscenity of enormous proportions. Peek behind the frontage when the site is under construction and you will see a Goldfinger’s lair, the urban equivalent of the arch villain’s Kentucky stud farm in the 1964 film. Not only will the whole city block have been cleared but ground will have been dug out to sink many levels of basement into the bowels of the earth.

Goldfinger himself – I refer to the Modernist architect Ernő Goldfinger, not his fictional namesake – would have been horrified. Honesty to structure was a sacred tenet to followers of the so-called International Style, which arrived from the Continent in the 1930s. Applied decoration was anathema.

Propagandists for the style borrowed the language of A.W.N. Pugin, the Victorian architect. He had put welly into the Gothic Revival by making it a moral cause. Pointed (i.e. medieval) architecture was structurally consistent, Christian and pure; places such as Ramsgate, where, being a contrarian, he had chosen to live, were debased by stucco that hid shoddy brickwork and was painted to look like stone.

After the Second World War, the tenets of Modernism went unchallenged. However much ordinary people were attached to terraced streets, the only morally valid building technology was that which used steel, concrete and glass. The march of progress had ordained it. Pastiche was verboten.

Although the collapse of a new 22-storey tower called Ronan Point following a gas explosion in 1968 revealed (literal) cracks in the premise, the mood among developers and planners did not change until 1984, when the then Prince Charles articulated what many of the public were already saying to each other in his famous Carbuncle speech. The Prince had read David Watkin’s Morality and Architecture, published in 1977, which showed that the intellectual foundations of Modernism were piffle.

This created a problem. If Modernism fell foul of conservationists, whose voice was now being heard, how were developers to provide the sort of office buildings their clients wanted, whilst also making oodles of money? They couldn’t simply flatten pre-existing streets as they had done before.

The answer? Façadism. Keep the fronts and fool the public into believing that Dickens or Boswell might have strolled past and found nothing amiss, while building a completely new frame at the back. This became established practice wherever historic building considerations raised their heads. Conservation areas have been, in many cases, turned into shells as a consequence.

It is – no surprise – all driven by money, not only the desire to avoid costly delays in the planning process but the iniquitous Treasury policy of charging VAT on works to old buildings while giving a free pass to new construction. It works like this: developers propose to knock down a listed building; general horror erupts; they then accept a compromise which involves keeping the façade while pocketing the VAT exemption because, after all, the result will be 99 per cent new.

This scam offends as the holiest of 21st-century objectives: the reduction of carbon emissions. Old buildings lock up large quantities of embedded carbon, whereas new ones are massive emitters; so it’s better to work with old structures as far as possible.

I will admit that façadism does have one small virtue: it keeps the street line intact. How grateful we were then, as Britain emerged blinking from the Modernist hegemony, for that small saving grace. Streets are essential for happiness. They are where people meet each other. Visitors can instantly “read” them, seeing where the shops are, finding the front doors.

Stand-alone Modernist buildings were surrounded by soulless piazzas, where rubbish drifted like tumbleweed. Now, incredibly, numerous architects are working with the street, not against it. Not just traditionalists such as ADAM Architecture and Ben Pentreath, in the Charles III camp, but young firms such as Peter Barber Architects and Trevor Morriss of SPPARC.

On an unintended drive around South London earlier this week, after being misdirected by sat nav, I saw many examples. Come on guys, we can afford more self-belief. Keep old buildings intact, build new ones that make decent streets. Our cities deserve it.

data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: Security guards told not to stop shoplifters
data.description:
Security guards are being told not to stop shoplifters over health and safety fears despite a crime wave sweeping the country.

Union bosses are telling their members to “watch, report and be an expert witness for police”, but are explicitly ordering them not to intervene against theft.

The Security Industry Federation (SIF) has told its members, “do not put yourselves in harm’s way for those who may not support you afterwards”.

The union’s general secretary Daniel Garnham said that while this diktat went “against the instincts and principles of many good security professionals... someone else’s stock is not more important than your safety, your career or your licence.”

He added: “Security workers cannot continue carrying all of the personal and professional risk while others avoid responsibility.”

The retail union Usdaw, which has 360,000 members, is also telling its members “not to intervene against potentially dangerous criminals and to keep themselves safe.”

GMB – one of Britain’s biggest unions representing thousands of security guards – said that until better regulation was in place, “retailers cannot expect security guards to put themselves at physical risk just to protect their bottom lines.”

Retailers are facing a shoplifting epidemic in Britain, with offences surging by 133pc over the past four years on the back of a cost of living crisis.

Supermarkets have come under fire for so-called “deter-not-detain” policies designed to keep staff safe, with videos circulating on social media showing criminals walking out of shops unchallenged.

Waitrose sacked a member of staff who had worked for the supermarket for 17 years after he stopped a suspected shoplifter stealing a bag of luxury Easter eggs, it emerged this month.

Morrisons was also criticised for firing one of its store managers after he tackled a prolific thief last week in breach of the supermarket’s policy.

The unions’ advice is thought to be the first widespread instance of security guards officially being told not to take action.

It threatens to undermine Sir Keir Starmer’s efforts to clamp down on rising crime as he seeks to reset his premiership in the wake of the Mandelson scandal and in the run-up to the local elections.

The SIF explained it had adopted its stance and will retain it until the supermarket industry comes up with a unified policy on how to tackle shoplifters.

Mr Garnham said guards were “expected to deter theft, protect staff and reassure the public” but that workers at different shops were being given completely different instructions.

“Across the UK there is no joined-up approach,” he said, which was putting guards in “an impossible position”.

“Security workers moving between different stores and contracts are often expected to work to different rules in different places. In one site they may be told to observe and report only. In another they may be expected to stop and detain offenders. In another they may face disciplinary action for trying to intervene at all.

“This is unfair on workers, unsafe for the public and completely unacceptable in a profession that plays such an important role in keeping people safe,” he said.

He added that he had written to supermarkets “to push for a joined-up approach and to ensure clear and consistent instructions would be put in place across the board.”

Meanwhile, GMB said it has been trying to raise industry standards for a year with the Security Industry Authority (SIA), the arm’s-length government regulator for the security sector, but claimed it has been ignored.

Guidance from the SIA on intervening against shoplifters says guards “do not have any special powers that extend beyond the powers of any other citizen”. It says “security operatives may use such force as is reasonable in the circumstances in the prevention of crime or the citizen’s arrest of an offender”, but adds that any force used “must be necessary to the circumstances and proportionate to the threat”.

It will be a blow to the Prime Minister, who claimed on Monday “the tide could be turning” against shoplifting as he set out new laws to reduce spiralling crime.

In an address to the Usdaw shopworkers’ union, Sir Keir said: “It’s disgraceful that people just working in their shop have to take abuse from customers.

“It’s disgraceful that people feel sick to the stomach thinking about how they’re going to get through the day, and it’s disgraceful that people can have their lives and livelihoods ruined by persistent shop theft.”

He said the Government had put an extra 3,000 neighbourhood police officers on the streets and scrapped the “ridiculous” rule which left theft of goods worth less than £200 “not properly investigated” by police.

“That was a shoplifters’ charter, and we’ve ended it and not before time,” he said.

“We’ve toughened up punishment too. We’re giving police stronger powers, making the abuse and assault of retail workers a specific crime and giving you the same protections as emergency workers.”

Sir Keir said he was “not blind to how big this challenge is” but said the number of people charged had gone up 17pc in the latest statistics and shop theft was down.

The latest Office for National Statistics data show shoplifting offences fell slightly last year, down from 516,611 in 2024 to 509,566 in 2025.

“It’s only slightly down, but the tide could be turning,” Sir Keir claimed.

Lucy Whing, crime policy lead at the British Retail Consortium, said: “Retail crime remains a major issue, and retailers have invested £5bn over the past five years to help tackle this issue, with security guards playing a key role in preventing crime. Retailers are continuing to work closely with police, government and the security industry to tackle the scourge of theft and violence.”

The SIA was approached for comment.
