data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: ‘My husband is in prison but BT won’t let me cancel his TNT Sports subscription’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Ruth Emery, our consumer champion, is here to help. To contact Ruth, click here*

Dear Ruth,

My husband was sentenced to prison in 2024. His crime was committed before I met him, and I knew nothing about it until his arrest. It has been devastating for me.

Since then, I’ve had problems cancelling the TNT Sports package on our BT account. It’s in his name, although I am a named contact and I pay the bills.

BT said that “named contacts” could not change subscriptions but that it would accept a power of attorney. My husband set up an ordinary power of attorney before he went to prison, but BT told me it would only accept a “lasting” power of attorney (LPA).

As a retired lawyer specialising in powers of attorney, I know the “ordinary” type (also known as “general”) should be adequate for any business matters, particularly as it is only needed during the time he is in prison and he is not suffering from any lack of capacity. However, BT doesn’t accept them. I made a complaint, but it was not upheld.

The only way my husband could make an LPA is for a lawyer to be authorised to go into the prison to see him and get the paperwork signed. That would be very expensive and would take many months.

Foolishly, I tried again to reach a solution with BT recently. The £22.31 monthly charge that I’m paying for a service I’m not using was going to increase by £2 a month at the end of March. I spent one hour and 45 minutes on the phone to BT, and was told the only way I could get rid of the sports package was for them to cancel my husband’s account, at a cancellation cost of almost £600, and open an account in my own name.

Very stupidly, I agreed to do this, but then realised he would lose his email account, which is very important as all his legal and business communications come to it. Prisoners can’t access email, but I am monitoring the account with his permission.

So, I tried to undo the cancellation to put everything back where it was, even though it will involve high monthly costs. The person I spoke to managed to cancel the new account in my name, and I was told my husband’s account would be reinstated.

Then, someone from BT’s “retention team” phoned asking to speak to my husband. I explained again why this wasn’t possible. The caller said he could say nothing to me because I was not a named contact on the account, even though everyone else at BT so far has confirmed that I am.

The worry about this is making me ill. I need a telephone and broadband service and the email address is vital. It is horrible having to go through the trauma of talking about a husband in prison every time I try to speak to BT. Every call is such a nightmare that I have to spend days psyching myself up to undergo the ordeal, and every time things just get worse, not better.

Please could you help me?

SJ, Gloucestershire

Dear reader,

I can only imagine the pain of seeing your husband imprisoned, and then having to repeatedly explain his absence to unsympathetic companies like BT.

Firms have previously been criticised for the way they treat relatives of deceased customers (and many have now improved their processes), but it seems that other scenarios, such as dealing with the loved ones of incarcerated customers, are still causing misery.

I hope this column highlights the importance of companies having a framework in place when these distressing situations arise, so that compassionate and practical solutions can be found swiftly.

You deliberately arranged an ordinary power of attorney for your husband. This is similar to an LPA, but crucially, it is for a temporary period. Someone might want to give a friend or family member an ordinary power of attorney to manage their affairs if, for example, they are physically injured (but still mentally sound), are abroad for a long period of time or are in prison.

An LPA, on the other hand, can be used when someone loses the mental capacity to make their own decisions. You didn’t want to create this power of attorney as it might make it difficult for your husband to resume dealing with his affairs when he’s released in a few years’ time.

You told me: “I think because LPAs now have such a high profile, staff are often not trained to recognise and deal with ordinary powers of attorney. BT has not suggested the power of attorney is invalid, only that it chooses not to deal with them.”

You refer to your actions as “foolish” and “stupid”, but you’re being too hard on yourself.

Prisoners can only call specific numbers belonging to friends and family, and not organisations like BT, so it really is up to you to cancel this TV package – and you have been battling for 18 months to make the telecoms giant listen to you. Occasionally, it suggested a solution, and understandably, you followed its advice.

I was furious with BT on your behalf, as I have been with other companies that have failed to put things right despite the customer complaining for well over a year.

(Yes, I’m talking about Barclays and the Post Office, which I detailed in a previous column. And British Gas in another.)

Thankfully, a day after I intervened, BT called you to apologise, and said it would cancel TNT Sports. You were told that a cancellation with 30 days’ notice could have been placed by yourself, and the firm would look at retraining its staff.

As a gesture of goodwill, it said it would refund six months of the sports charges. I urged BT to reconsider, given you have been trying to cancel it for longer than this. It agreed to a refund of charges for a full year and credited your account with £270. After more nagging by me, BT also sent you flowers to apologise.

A BT spokesman said: “We are very sorry that [your reader] had difficulty cancelling TNT Sports on her husband’s account. To acknowledge her experience, we have provided her with a goodwill gesture, and the complaint has been resolved.”

– Ruth

Consumer Champion Katie Morley is currently on maternity leave. Read her previous investigations here.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph’s processing of your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Silicon Valley faces chatbot rationing as AI computing crunch bites
data.description:
For a company that was only recently viewed as a scrappy underdog in the AI arms race, Anthropic has quickly become a victim of its own success.

The explosive growth in demand for AI coding bots has forced the $380bn (£280bn) Silicon Valley lab to start rationing access to its most popular tools, leaving paying customers fuming.

“Claude Code has become unusable,” moaned one user earlier this month. “I tried to stay. I couldn’t. Just cancelled,” said another.

In late March, Anthropic admitted it had started limiting the amount of tokens – effectively the building blocks of its AI answers – that it offers users during peak hours across its subscription plans, which can cost as much as £180 per month. This week, it added it was testing dropping its coding tool from its popular Claude Pro tier, which costs £18 per month.

The decision to suddenly ration access to its AI has led to users complaining that Claude will abruptly stop tasks or take shortcuts that result in botched work.

“Rate limits, which were generous in the past, are barely enough to get serious work done,” says Soumitra Shukla, a research fellow at Harvard Business School’s Artificial Intelligence Institute. He says his Claude now often refuses simple tasks, such as reading a PDF, with the bot complaining “they’re too big so they don’t even attempt it”.

It is not just consumers feeling the pinch. For businesses, this month Anthropic also started charging companies on a pay-as-you-go basis, rather than offering them a flat corporate fee with a bundled amount of tokens.

Soaring demand struggles

Anthropic’s head of growth, Amol Avasare, said the AI app was trying to handle an explosion in demand. Users have been running semi-autonomous AI agents, even while they are away from their desks or sleeping, burning through their credits.

Some Silicon Valley companies have even set up leader boards that monitor which staff can blitz through as many AI tokens as possible, a trend called “tokenmaxxing”. According to data from Epoch AI, the number of people using Anthropic in the US climbed 40pc in March alone.

“The way people actually use a Claude subscription has changed fundamentally,” Anthropic’s Avasare said. “Usage has changed a lot and our current plans weren’t built for this.”

Led by Dario Amodei, an AI safety advocate, Anthropic’s Claude can answer questions or write code, competing against OpenAI’s ChatGPT. 

So far this year, it has secured an edge over the competition and enjoyed surging demand from business customers. 

Anthropic’s annual recurring revenue – a preferred estimate of sales in Silicon Valley – climbed about $6bn to $19bn in February alone, according to Amodei.

But Anthropic has also found itself butting up against the physical limits of AI computing power as it scrambles to build vast data centres around the world. Despite signing deals worth more than $150bn to buy computing capacity, including a $100bn deal with Amazon this week, most of these data sites will not be online for years.

That shortfall has left Anthropic struggling to keep up with OpenAI.

Richard Windsor, a technology analyst, says Anthropic made an error by failing to buy up enough computing power and now finds itself “more constrained” than its main rival, OpenAI.

“OpenAI has a computing and GPU advantage versus Anthropic,” says Dan Ives, of Wedbush Securities. “Anthropic has the best [AI model] – now it’s about capacity.”

Azeem Azhar, the founder of the technology newsletter Exponential View, agrees.

“My personal experience is that Claude Opus, their flagship model, felt ‘dumber’ at times in the past month,” says Azhar.

‘I’m done with Anthropic’

Despite the technical advantages of its AI tools, even major corporate customers have threatened to quit Anthropic as they run into unexpected limits on their use or find the chatbot changing their project halfway through to cut corners.

“I think I’m completely done with Anthropic at this point,” wrote Dan Woods, the vice president of AI at CVS Health, the world’s second-largest healthcare company.

Some analysts have even speculated that Anthropic has held back the launch of Mythos, a new coding and cyber-security tool, because it simply would not be able to service the demand.

While Anthropic claimed it had limited the release of the AI owing to concerns about its potential hacking capabilities, Ben Thompson, a veteran analyst, wrote: “Anthropic is already short on compute serving its current models ... making Mythos more widely available would make the situation worse.”

One issue with launching the most powerful AI tools is that they cost more to run. Anthropic has priced Mythos at up to $125 per million output tokens, five times that of its next most expensive AI, suggesting its new, powerful bots use far more computing power.

Unfortunately for Anthropic, there are no quick fixes for its capacity crunch. “I don’t think the bottleneck eases any time soon as long as it continues to grow as fast as it is,” says Windsor.

According to a report by analysts at Sightline Climate, as many as 50pc of data centres supposedly coming online this year are behind schedule or may not launch at all. Planning, logistical and financial constraints have caused a number of projects to hit pause.

Meanwhile, the price of memory chips, crucial for building AI data centres, have spiked with manufacturing shortages expected for the rest of the year. The cost of renting the most powerful Nvidia chips has also climbed almost 50pc in two months.

Azhar, of Exponential View, estimates the infrastructure gap is a “12 to 18-month problem, not a permanent one”.

For now, OpenAI appears to have avoided the crunch. In a post on X, plainly aimed at its main rival, OpenAI wrote this week: “We’re planning for 30 GW (gigawatt) of compute by 2030. 

“A milestone that scales with the rapidly accelerating demand for intelligent systems.” 

The company has forecast that it will spend up to $600bn on computing power this decade.

Yet even the ChatGPT-maker has had to rein in some programmes, including shutting down its power-sapping Sora video app. Sarah Friar, OpenAI’s finance chief, admitted that it too was making some trade-offs “on things we’re not pursuing because we don’t have enough compute”.

Compute ‘choke point’

It is also not just the new AI labs that are affected by the computing shortfall.

Sir Demis Hassabis, the Google DeepMind chief, acknowledged in February a need to carefully prioritise AI work to avoid hitting the limits of its computing reserves, telling CNBC there was a compute “choke point”.

This week, Microsoft’s GitHub, its coding business that offers access to its Copilot AI bot, blocked new sign-ups for some of its subscription plans.

“More customers are hitting usage limits designed to maintain service reliability,” said Joe Binder, GitHub’s product chief. “Without further action, service quality degrades for everyone.”

All this may be an annoyance for AI enthusiasts who will find themselves paying more for their favourite chatbots.

But for Anthropic, navigating the capacity crunch could be existential.

The company has been linked with a $1tn public flotation and a shortage of compute could hamstring its growth.

Yet Anthropic’s Amodei has also warned that if the company agrees to reserve hundreds of billions of dollars in computing power every year, it could have a devastating financial impact if its growth plans fall short.

“There’s no hedge on Earth that could stop me from going bankrupt if I buy that much compute,” he said.

That means Claude fans will need to accept their daily ration of AI – or be prepared to open their wallets.

data.title: Britain in talks to sell Rolls-Royce mini nuclear reactors to Sweden
data.description:
Britain is in advanced talks about a deal that would see Rolls-Royce supply pioneering mini nuclear power plants to Sweden.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, said the discussions with Stockholm were among several taking place between the Government and European allies as part of efforts to strengthen energy security in light of the Iran war.

Rolls-Royce’s small modular reactors (SMRs) are seen as a breakthrough because they could be produced in factories and then assembled on site, dramatically reducing build times compared to larger plants.

The first ones are set to be built in the UK and the Czech Republic, but Mr Kyle said he was currently touring European capitals in an effort to secure further orders.

The Business Secretary told The Telegraph: “I’m in advanced talks with Sweden. I’m also beating the drum with other European countries and further afield.

“I see this as central to our growth agenda back home. It’s about the re-industrialisation of the British economy, placing it at the forefront of highly innovative, precise and growth-orientated, highly profitable manufacturing.”

It comes after Rolls-Royce won a design competition run by Great British Energy – Nuclear last year.

That will see Rolls-Royce supply the mini reactors for three power plants being built in Anglesey, North Wales, with the potential room for another five on the site.

The company has also signed an agreement to build another six in Temelín for the Czech state energy company ČEZ, while in Sweden it is in the final running to supply its SMR technology to state energy giant Vattenfall.

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, said the Czech deal would unlock “a significant programme of work”.

Mr Kyle said he was now pushing for the Derby-based engineering giant to secure further work on the Continent as the Middle East crisis prompted fresh worries about energy security.

Those potentially interested could even include Germany, where large nuclear plants have been phased out but ministers have recently expressed an interest in SMRs.

Mr Kyle is poised to travel to Berlin next week and is expected to raise the subject during his visit.

Last year, The Telegraph revealed Sweden had approached allies including the UK about the potential to place a bulk order for SMRs.

Ebba Busch, the Swedish deputy prime minister, said her country wanted to band together to buy at least 10 to 15 reactors in an effort to cut costs and share expertise.

Rolls-Royce has said it can get the cost per SMR down to an estimated $3bn (£2.2bn) per unit once production is up and running, although the technology remains unproven.

Tufan Erginbilgic, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce, has estimated that the world will need 400 SMRs by 2050 and that the British company has a chance to dominate the market.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Major housebuilder almost halves in value as Iran war hits profits
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest housebuilders has almost halved in value after bosses unveiled contingency measures to avoid a debt crunch.

Crest Nicholson told investors on Tuesday that it had kickstarted emergency talks with lenders to secure “temporary” relief on its banking covenants.

The announcement, which also included a profit warning the housebuilder blamed on war in Iran, led to more than £124m being wiped off its value as shares fell by as much as 45pc.

Martyn Clark, the chief executive, said Crest had been hit by the conflict in the Middle East, fuelling the prospect of a “prolonged higher interest rate environment, renewed cost pressures and a deterioration in consumer confidence”.

This is likely to hamper sales for Crest, which now expects to generate earnings before interest and tax of just £5m to £15m for the full year, dramatically below January’s guidance of at least £32m.

As a result it is seeking relief on its debts.

The builder has cut its yearly sales guidance to between 1,400 and 1,500 homes, down from as many as 1,700. Meanwhile, it expects net debt to reach up to £120m, revised up from £15m-£65m.

It also warned of mounting construction costs triggered by soaring energy prices.

Bricks, blocks and steel are among the construction materials that are heavily reliant on energy for production.

Crest Nicholson is the latest in a string of housebuilders facing mounting financial pressures caused by the Gulf conflict, as higher inflation is likely to increase borrowing costs for buyers, hurting demand.

Berkeley Group also recently sounded the alarm by halting new land purchases and scaling back investment in its building projects.

Crest warned that new enquiries had begun to fall, noting that the South has seen the sharpest drop in activity.

Separately, the company cut its land sales revenue forecast to around £40m, less than the £75m to £100m it had targeted, and said it no longer expected to make “material” profit from selling land before its financial year ends in October.

Mr Clark said the housebuilder was moving “quickly” to shore up its balance sheet, insisting that it was well-placed to weather the uncertainty.

“We are doing what needs to be done to navigate this uncertainty to best position the business to deliver the attractive medium-term opportunity,” he said.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

data.title: Hogwarts Express train risks being derailed by health and safety
data.description:
A steam train that has been carrying Harry Potter fans through some of Britain’s most spectacular countryside for decades may be nearing the end of the line.

The Hogwarts Express usually operates twice daily through the summer in western Scotland, passing landmarks including the Glenfinnan Viaduct made famous by the films.

The train itself also appeared regularly in filming as the boy wizard and his companions were shown making the journey from King’s Cross to Hogwarts Castle for the new school term.

Now, health and safety rules are putting the future of the service in doubt after its 70-year-old carriages depicted in the blockbuster franchise were outlawed because their doors did not have central locking.

West Coast Railways, which runs the service and is the biggest operator of steam trains on the national network, said the newer carriages it has been forced to use by the Office of Rail and Road (ORR) have been unpopular with tourists.

They are also a drain on cash because their power-hungry air conditioners cannot be run off a steam locomotive, requiring a heavy diesel engine to be attached to the back.

The train, which should have begun its 2026 timetable on April 1, has yet to run this year. West Coast is seeking permission from the ORR to bring back the original coaches, known as Mk1s, which require only a small window to be opened to provide ventilation.

James Shuttleworth of West Coast said rejection of the proposals could jeopardise the viability of the train, which is formally known as the Jacobite and has been running 41 miles between Fort William and Mallaig since 1984.

He said: “I don’t know what we would do. We were losing money working like that and you don’t go into business to lose money.

“The Jacobite needs to have the Mk1s to carry on. They’re the best vehicles for tourists and general passengers. You open the windows and you’ve got fresh air, and they’ve still got the comfy seats, big windows and all of that.”

West Coast said it had committed to fitting the 1950s-era carriages with central locks as part of its application to the ORR.

However, with 120 vehicles to be converted, the work is expected to cost upwards of £5m and could take years, raising doubts about whether the proposals will gain approval.

Maria Woods, owner of Haggard Alley, a Harry Potter-themed shop in Mallaig, said delays to the Jacobite service this year have caused a slump in business worse than at the height of Covid. Her other outlet in Fort William has also been temporarily closed.

She said: “We’ve lost a whole month of custom already, which is astronomical for us. Businesses in Mallaig are really suffering and it’s a huge worry.

“We are operating when we can, but without the footfall, it’s a very scary time and it’s not bringing in enough to cover costs.

“It’s the same in the cafes, and I had a joiner in the other day saying people are cancelling jobs because money isn’t coming in from the train. I just pray that some sort of compromise can be reached for July and August.”

Mr Shuttleworth of West Coast said that because of their configuration, the number of seats on the Jacobite had to be reduced during a trial of the new carriages last summer. Adding more coaches is not an option because of limits on train length on the West Highland line, he said.

Mr Shuttleworth added: “The newer carriages require an electrical supply.

So you’ve got to have a 2,000 horsepower, 120-ton diesel locomotive on the back, which is a very expensive dead weight.

“If that goes wrong, you’ll roast because you can’t open the windows. The Mk 1s don’t have air conditioning or anything like that. They’re simple vehicles and they’re simple to maintain.

“It also means that we can use a more diverse range of coaches that we have got, such as our Pullmans and the like.”

Mr Shuttleworth said West Coast had accepted that it could not carry on without new locks but was seeking an exemption to operate the old stock without them this summer.

He said: “We need to do a bit of research on it and then we will get on with fitting it. Other companies got two years to fit a dozen coaches. I’m hardly thinking we’re going to be getting twenty years to fit 120 coaches, but a reasonable period of time.”

He said an announcement on the Jacobite website saying that agreement had been reached with the ORR had been “over egged” and had been intended to indicate that West Coast had agreed to install central locks.

The ORR said proposals for the train remain under assessment.

A spokesman added: “We note that West Coast Railways recognises the need for central door locking on the Mk1 carriages it operates. However, work remains to establish the feasibility, detail and delivery timeline of the proposed approach.”

data.title: Transport watchdog director investigated over train ticket expenses
data.description:
Britain’s taxpayer-funded transport watchdog is investigating a member of its board over allegations he wrongly obtained student railcard discounts and double-claimed travel expenses.

Transport Focus, which represents passengers and is funded mainly by a grant from the Department for Transport, is reviewing payments to Rob Wilson, a non-executive director at the watchdog and a former Tory minister. The Cabinet Office is also investigating his expenses.

Records released under the Freedom of Information Act and seen by The Telegraph show that Mr Wilson claimed the cost of two journeys from both Transport Focus and one of his other publicly funded employers.

In November last year, the records show Mr Wilson claimed a £71.30 train ticket and £26 of parking from both Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission, a quango sponsored by the Cabinet Office, where he is deputy chairman.

He told each organisation he was travelling for a separate event related to his work for them. Mr Wilson reported to Transport Focus that the expenses were related to a board meeting and to the Social Mobility Commission that they were to attend a “higher education summit”.

The records show he submitted the same picture of the receipt for the Reading to London return ticket for both claims.

Mr Wilson also claimed twice for a £68.20 Reading to London return ticket and £26 parking charge in September 2024. On this occasion, he charged the expenses to Transport Focus and the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), an organisation funded from water bills, where he is chairman.

Again, Mr Wilson gave each organisation different reasons for the expenses. He told Transport Focus that he had travelled for a board meeting and the CCW that he had a meeting with the chairman of Anglian Water.

 The Telegraph last month reported on Mr Wilson’s expenses records at the CCW, which showed he had obtained discounts on two train tickets worth a third of their cost using a 16-25 railcard.

The discount brought the cost of first-class tickets under the cost of standard class, making them allowable under the CCW’s expenses policy. The organisation said the former MP, who is 61, had selected the option by mistake in two instances.

His expenses records at Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission indicate he made similar errors in his other roles.

He billed Transport Focus for £38.29 last April after booking a journey with a 16-25 railcard discount. The same month, he also charged the Cabinet Office, which sponsors the Social Mobility Commission, £101.49 for another journey.

Transport Focus, which last year paid Mr Wilson a fee of £14,000, confirmed this weekend that his expenses claims are under investigation. Claiming a railcard discount fraudulently is a criminal offence and attracts a fine of up to £1,000.

A spokesman said: “We have a process ongoing which reviewed information we received in late March. The process is continuing, reflecting information that has emerged more recently. It would not be appropriate to comment further until that work is complete.”

A Cabinet Office spokesman said: “We were unaware of a duplicate claim by Mr Wilson with Transport Focus until the issue was raised by The Telegraph. We will be investigating this further.”

Lawyers for Mr Wilson said the railcard discounts again “arose from a mistake in his use of the relevant booking app” and its repetition was the result of a default setting.

They added that his double claiming was accidental and that he had intended to split the cost between his employers. Mr Wilson’s lawyers said he was investigating the matter and would ensure that any overpayment was reimbursed.

Mr Wilson, who left Parliament in 2017, also had difficulty selecting the right options while booking flights, according to his Transport Focus records. In 2020 and 2023, he booked and expensed British Airways flights as the Honourable Robert Wilson and The Right Honourable Robert Wilson.

The former honorific is typically used by sitting MPs to refer to each other in parliamentary debates but otherwise mostly applied to the children of viscounts. The Right Honourable is reserved for senior peers and sitting MPs who are members of the Privy Council, on which Mr Wilson never served.

Mr Wilson’s lawyers said the title was mistakenly added to his flight bookings by an administrator. He “had since engaged with British Airways to ensure that this error is corrected”.

It is not the first time that Mr Wilson has encountered controversy over his expenses. In the MPs’ expenses scandal, he was revealed to have claimed nine pence from taxpayers for a 352-yard car journey. He defended the claim, saying the cost of travel “does add up”.

data.title: Self-checkouts fuelling shoplifting crisis, says M&S boss
data.description:
The chairman of Marks & Spencer claims self-checkouts are fuelling a rise in shoplifting among “good, honest people”.

Archie Norman told The Telegraph that a boom in self-service checkouts at supermarkets had broken the “human link” between shoppers and retailers.

He said: “When normally good, honest people come in and they’re buying their shopping and it doesn’t scan, and there’s nobody manning the checkouts, they’re saying: ‘It’s not my fault and I don’t have much time so if I can’t get my strawberries through, I’ll just put them in my basket’.”

M&S has added hundreds of self-service machines across its stores. It said in 2023 that it had installed 800 of the tills in just 12 months to help it to reach its £150m cost-saving target.

The supermarket began adding self-checkouts into changing rooms in 2024. Last year, it started trialling hi-tech self-checkouts for clothes that identify what is in someone’s basket without the need to scan barcodes.

Mr Norman said retailers did not “have to bring back in-person checkouts” to clamp down on theft. However, he said: “It does mean you’ve got to make the technology easier for people to use.”

His comments follow warnings that crime rates are picking up again across British high streets.

M&S said earlier this month that attacks were becoming “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive” after mobs of youths were captured on video raiding one of its stores in Clapham, London.

Thinus Keeve, the M&S retail director, criticised Sir Sadiq Khan, the London Mayor, for failing to get a grip on crime in the capital, claiming the situation was “getting worse, not better”.

Mr Norman said on Thursday that self-checkouts were not to blame for the riots in Clapham. He blamed uncooperative police forces for failing to intervene against more prolific shoplifters, who he said were “clearing shelves to feed a habit”.

Mr Norman said: “When you have gangs of kids coming in and sweeping the shelves, that’s a police event and it requires an active police response.

“When something like that starts to become common it says to everybody, including ordinary citizens, that it’s not safe.”

Retail chiefs have been urging police to take a more active role in tackling store crime. Simon Roberts, the chief executive of Sainsbury’s, said on Thursday that it would be “a really good thing to see” more police walking around its stores.

Mr Roberts said: “It would make the point that this issue is really serious, it really matters and it’s really top of the agenda.”

Official data this week showed more than half a million shoplifting incidents were reported to police last year. The 509,566 offences represented a 1pc fall from 2024’s level, according to Office for National Statistics figures.

However, retail bosses argue that these figures do not reflect the full picture, given they only reflect those cases that are flagged to the police. Low prosecution rates have meant some retailers have stopped reporting shoplifting incidents.

Lucy Whing, at the British Retail Consortium, said the industry group’s own data indicated roughly 10 times as many thefts were committed against stores, with 5.5 million incidents last year.

She said the rise in organised crime was “particularly worrying as gangs systematically target one store after another across the country”. She called on the police to “make full use” of new powers to crack down on retail crime.

Rising cost of shoplifting

Lord Richard Walker, the Iceland chairman, on Thursday claimed the supermarket was losing “£20m a year on shoplifting”.

Lord Walker told LBC that the rise in shoplifting was fuelling the cost of living crisis by eating into workers’ wages and “investment in lowering prices”.

He also called on the Information Commissioner’s Office to “be on our side” by making greater use of facial recognition technology.

Some retailers have responded to a rise in shoplifting by installing so-called “receipt gates” which require shoppers to scan their receipts to exit.

However, Mr Norman ruled out such a move at the Retail Technology Show in London on Thursday, telling audiences that installing gates would contradict the retailer’s philosophy.

He said: “That’s not us. M&S is a trusted environment – and a safe place to shop.”

data.title: If Scott Mills was the dreary uncle, Sara Cox is like your fun, fag-smoking auntie
data.description:
Radio 2’s relationship with its Breakfast Show hosts in the past few years has been a bit like a Hollywood romcom. The unlucky-in-love radio station has bounced between the warm and mumsy (Zoe Ball) and the dogged but dreary (Scott Mills), only to find that the perfect match was there all along – the faithful, funny best pal who’s always on hand with a glass of wine. So Radio 2 stalwart Sara Cox, after Mills’s precipitous fall from grace, has been given the keys to the nation’s most prestigious radio show. It’s a sensible move from the BBC, which must be wondering why it overlooked Cox for Mills in the first place.

That’s not to say that Cox is a “safe pair of hands” (we said that, derisorily, about Mills when he was appointed). She began her career on Channel 4’s controversial late-night “ladette” programme The Girlie Show, a lewd and loud cavalcade of smut, booze and lads with their tops off. As a presenter on The Big Breakfast and then the Radio 1 Breakfast Show, the earthy Boltonian was a tabloid poster girl for lairy late nights. However, Cox has emerged from the Wild West of the Nineties/Noughties media world as one of the good guys.

Her journey has mirrored that of many Radio 2 listeners (indeed, Cox could be called the apex “Mood Mum”). Since the wild-child years, she’s married, had children, and become an author, as well as hosting BBC Two’s literary show Between the Covers. Last month she raised £11.5m for Children in Need by running 135 miles in five days. However, what gives Cox her edge is that she has never pretended to be squeaky clean. This is, after all, the woman who, on Radio 1, gave us details of how she lost her virginity in a field of sheep. The skeletons in her closet were all out partying till the small hours in the late 1990s. Cox is, in other words, normal.

Having been a stand-in on the Breakfast Show many times, as well as spending years as an exceedingly popular host of Radio 2’s Drive Time slot, we know what we’ll get with Cox in the mornings. Unlike Mills, she won’t overthink it and she won’t be anything other than herself – lively, a little fruity, a bit messy, extremely likeable. Of all the probable candidates for the role, Cox was the one who you would most like to go for a pint with – like your favourite fag-smoking auntie. She is, in many ways, BBC Radio’s Claudia Winkleman – a fizzy, impish presence who always played second fiddle to the more polished presenters but who bided their time and got their reward. From the summer, mornings on Radio 2 will be that bit brighter.

data.title: Tories pledge to drop blanket 20mph speed limits
data.description:
The Conservatives have pledged to scrap so-called “blanket” 20mph speed limits across the country.

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said on Thursday that Left-wing councils have turned 20mph into the “national speed of bureaucracy”.

Vowing they would be eliminated by Conservative-led councils, he said: “We are ending blanket limits that slow whole towns to a crawl while doing little for real safety.

“Town halls have spent years waging a war on the very people who get up early, drive to work and keep the economy turning.

“Only the Conservatives are prepared to end the war on drivers, rip up the blanket bans, and get Britain moving again for a stronger economy.”

Existing 20mph measures will be reviewed, Mr Holden said, with 30mph limits restored on roads where 20mph limits are widely ignored.

The announcement comes before the council elections on May 7.

Labour-run Wales is the main example of a blanket 20mph speed limit being imposed on Britain’s drivers.

The devolved government in Cardiff introduced and then partially abandoned a blanket 20mph policy on all local roads. It would become the single most unpopular law in the Senedd’s brief history.

It also cost the policy’s architect, Welsh Labour’s Lee Waters, his political career after he resigned in the wake of the backlash to its introduction.

Although Ken Skates, his successor, announced that the blanket ban would be lifted, few councils have raised their speed limits to 30mph. Only in areas outside schools and hospitals must the speed limit remain at 20mph in Wales.

The experiment left half a million people facing fines for breaking the lowered limit, as The Telegraph reported last year.

Current government guidance to councils, last updated in March 2024 – before Labour’s general election victory in July that year – says: “Traffic authorities should use the right speed limits in the right places. They should keep their speed limits under review and only introduce 20mph limits and zones in the right places, over time and with local support in urban areas and built-up village streets that are primarily residential.”

The Conservatives’ Plan for Drivers, published in 2023, set out the principle that 20mph limits should be targeted and evidence-led rather than imposed as “blanket measures”, a party spokesman said.

Mr Holden added: “The previous Conservative government was clear in our guidance that blanket approaches were wrong. Labour ignored it and their councils kept rolling them out in the teeth of public opposition.”

data.title: PPI 2.0: the claims machine trying to cash in on your car finance payout
data.description:
On social media, text and even by post, a message has been bombarding Britons: you could be owed thousands of pounds for mis-sold car loans.

A decision two years ago by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) to launch a review of motor finance deals triggered a gold rush to sign up drivers who may be able to claim compensation from banks and other lenders.

It has only intensified since a Supreme Court ruling last year that millions of motorists were mis-sold motor finance when car salesmen failed to disclose commissions paid by lenders.

Law firms and claims management companies (CMCs) have launched a marketing blitz to lure in clients with no-win, no-fee promises. If they do win, the spoils are rich: lawyers can land themselves up to 40pc of any payout.

The feeding frenzy builds upon business models refined during the heyday of the payment protection insurance (PPI) scandal, where millions of people were sold unneeded insurance. That eventually led to £36bn in compensation being paid by banks.

On Monday, law firms and CMCs are pitching themselves as a helping hand for drivers to receive what they are owed. Yet there are serious questions about just how necessary this cottage industry is.

At the end of last month, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) introduced its own completely free compensation scheme with total payouts expected to be £7.5bn. Unlike settlements secured through law firms or claims management businesses, drivers who take this route will keep 100pc of their compensation.

“Our scheme is free to use, and people do not need to go through a claims management company or a law firm,” a spokesman for the FCA said.

While there is no need, it is not explicitly prohibited and some law firms have dismissed the level of redress from the official FCA scheme as well below what consumers should be entitled to. They have instead pledged to go directly through the courts.

“Our position is that the scheme will work for some clients with lower value claims who want a quick outcome, but it represents a compromise, designed to limit the liability of the lenders rather than fairly compensate consumers,” says Robert Whitehead, the chairman and owner of Barings Law.

A case led by Barings against eight lenders is currently in the Court of Appeal and will determine whether firms can file class-action-style mass claims, or must do so individually.

Barings has been active in a range of mass-consumer claims, including data breaches, Dieselgate and aspects of the PPI scandal. The outcome of the motor finance case is hugely consequential for the law firm.

When Whitehead took over last year, Barings had just reported a £13m loss and its auditors were questioning whether it could survive. Court delays, and the existence of the free redress scheme, were both cited as potential points of concern by auditors.

Yet Whitehead argues Barings is sitting on £300m of potential revenue, much of it tied to car finance payouts.

(Whitehead also runs WHD Broking, which offers services including car finance. It is understood WHD Broking, whose primary source of income is providing consultancy to law firms, has never generated business in motor finance and references to the services were removed from the company’s website after Whitehead was contacted by The Telegraph.)

Some law firms do not appear to inform potential clients about the existence of the free FCA scheme at all, instead telling people they could win thousands of pounds at court – far more than the FCA has said the average claimant would be entitled to.

This is despite the fact that the Solicitors Regulation Authority (SRA), which regulates law firms, has said it expects any firm to inform its clients about the FCA’s scheme.

Sam Ward, the boss of Sentinel Legal, has often appeared in the media to explain the details of car finance. His firm’s website wrongly stated that the FCA’s scheme was yet to arrive. It tells potential clients that they could win an “average payout” of £5,318, despite the FCA expecting average redress payments to be around £830.

Sentinel’s website removed the reference to the FCA scheme still being in consultation after icontacted by The Telegraph.

Mr Ward said that Sentinel had informed clients of their available options and said the FCA’s compensation scheme was not the “sole or definitive route to redress for consumers”.

Sentinel said its payout figures were representative of what it believed clients could get through the court, and that it had already achieved wins above the FCA’s figure.

The SRA has so far taken no action against any law firm involved in soliciting car finance, though it has opened investigations into 71 firms.

But enforcement action in the CMC sector, which helps solicitors find car finance clients, suggests it is something of a wild west. The FCA has taken down more than 800 misleading adverts, including ones featuring Martin Lewis, the consumer champion, without permission. It has also forced three CMCs to reduce their unreasonable fees and blocked four more from taking on new clients.

“We have concerns about the bad actors in this space. They don’t help us or anybody else,” says a senior figure at one claims law firm that has itself reported rivals for misleading advertising.

The FCA said that if drivers chose to go to a law firm or CMC for compensation “they must be able to trust those firms to act in their best interests”.

In January, the FCA took the unprecedented step of publicly announcing an investigation into the Claims Protection Agency (TCPA), which promised to recover thousands of pounds for victims of alleged car finance mis-selling in adverts featuring Tyson Fury, the heavyweight boxer. 

The watchdog said it had “concerns about its advertising and sales tactics in relation to potential motor finance claims”.

The Claims Protection Agency said: “We have fully co-operated with the FCA in relation to its investigation, which we believe will exonerate our position. We wish to reassure consumers that we are fully able to continue to manage their compensation claims.”

TCPA received funding from Katch Investment Group, a London-based boutique hedge fund. Katch wound down its litigation fund last year, but past years suggested it was a highly profitable business. It reported a return of 19.1pc on parts of the fund in 2023, the last year that accounts were available.

Katch also provided funding to Consumer Rights Solicitors, which is heavily involved in motor finance litigation and PPI litigation. The law firm makes no mention of the existence of the FCA’s redress scheme on its website, saying only that it is “expected in 2026 at the earliest”.

Katch Investment Group and Consumer Rights Solicitors did not provide a response to a request for comment.

The City watchdog and the SRA have launched a joint taskforce to crack down on poor practice in motor finance claims.

It is not just advertising that is concerning the two regulators. Lenders have seen numerous instances of the same customer being claimed by multiple firms simultaneously, something regulators have told companies not to do.

In one case, 21 different CMCs and law firms were all representing the same client, vying for a share of a single redress payment.

Even as law firms and CMCs scramble for a slice of the motor finance payouts, the end of the scandal appears closer than ever.

Yet for these businesses, there will always be other scandals to pursue. In fact, the motor finance mis-selling scandal has its roots in the last big payday.

After PPI, law firms looked to other areas where such “unfair relationships” might have existed, leading them to motor finance. No doubt there will be another scandal around the bend – at least in the eyes of the claims industry.

After PPI, law firms looked to other areas where such “unfair relationships” might have existed, leading them to motor finance. No doubt there will be another scandal around the bend – at least in the eyes of the claims industry.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Act now on energy or grocery bills will rise, Sainsbury’s warns Reeves
data.description:
The cost of a weekly shop will rise unless Rachel Reeves helps food manufacturers with spiralling energy prices, the boss of Sainsbury’s has warned.

Simon Roberts, the chief executive of Britain’s second largest supermarket, urged the Chancellor to intervene with further support despite a separate warning from Marks & Spencer that it was “too late” to prevent prices rising.

Mr Roberts said: “The single biggest thing the Government could do to help support keeping food prices down is to make sure that energy costs in the industry are not rising faster.

“Now is the time for the Government to really look at what it can do in the food sector to keep those pressures contained as far as possible.”

Food inflation hit 3.7pc in March, but the Food and Drink Federation has warned that it could reach 10pc by the end of this year, fuelled by the Iran war.

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran has driven up the price of oil and gas, which will filter through into everything from greenhouse costs for vegetable growers to transportation of food and drink to supermarkets.

Archie Norman, the chairman of M&S, said: “You can’t see any trace of the Strait of Hormuz in shops today, but obviously cost increases are going to seep through the system.

“We all rely on sea freight, air freight and diesel. That’s our cost to carry.”

Speaking at a conference, he said: “I expect by September to see price inflation ticking up. Even if it stops today, it’s too late.”

Mr Roberts declined to speculate on how high inflation would go, but said: “There’s no doubt there’ll be pressure on food prices, given that energy is such a key component of what goes into the food we eat every day.”

He urged the Chancellor to broaden existing support packages for energy-intensive businesses to include food makers and retailers.

A government package unveiled last week will support more than 10,000 manufacturers in sectors such as steel and concrete. It will reduce their electricity bills by up to 25pc by axing green levies, though the support only takes effect next year.

Mr Roberts said widening support to cover the food industry would go “some way” to keeping a lid on rising grocery prices.

He said: “We ask for that to include food and food manufacturing parts of the industry ... We’ve made the point that anything we can do to bring down the cost of energy in the food system is going to help mitigate the impact of inflation.”

The comments come weeks after supermarket bosses met with the Chancellor to warn over the risk of rising food prices.

At the time, bosses told Ms Reeves that she needed to look at “domestic policy levels” to cool inflation. However, the Chancellor has yet to lay out support for the industry.

The Sainsbury’s chief said there were “a number of areas we discussed and I think it’s very clear that all of those need to be looked at and considered”.

He was speaking as the supermarket said its profits could drop to as low as £975m this year, down from £1.025bn last year, amid uncertainty over the Middle East conflict.

Mr Roberts said: “The conflict in the Middle East will impact both our customers and our business. The duration and extent of these impacts is very uncertain and this is reflected in our profit guidance.”

Shares in Sainsbury’s fell almost 5pc on the London Stock Exchange.

Separately, WH Smith warned over the impact of the war in Iran, slashing its profit forecasts for the year to the end of August to between £90m and £105m. It previously forecast profits to come in between £100m and £115m.

The retailer, which runs stores in airports, said it was now “taking a more cautious outlook reflecting the impact on passenger numbers and weaker consumer confidence”.

Shares in WH Smith plunged by 9pc.

It follows a warning from airlines this week over the risk of travel chaos this summer as they grapple with dwindling supplies of aviation fuel. The Government has been told to prepare for fuel shortages now if disruption to flights is to be avoided.

data.title: Meta to collect solar power from space
data.description:
Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta is planning to harvest solar energy from space to power its artificial intelligence data centres on Earth.

The Facebook and Instagram owner has signed a deal with US start-up Overview Energy to source up to one gigawatt of solar energy, the equivalent of powering 750,000 homes.

Meta has partnered with Overview as it prepares to launch a fleet of up to 1,000 satellites to collect the sun’s rays using solar panels in space.

This power will then be converted into low-intensity infrared beams, which can be directed at photovoltaic panels on Earth and converted into energy.

Solar power can be collected far more efficiently in space as the sun’s rays are not weakened by Earth’s atmosphere, nor are they affected by air pollution or cloud cover.

Satellites can also be positioned in orbits where they experience almost 24 hours of direct sunlight.

From space, they can transmit this power to areas currently in darkness, increasing the efficiency of existing solar farms.

The technology provides a boost to Silicon Valley giants as they construct vast AI data centres across the West.

Solar power from space could represent a constant source of clean power for tech companies, which have been at risk of breaching their environmental commitments by pursuing AI.

It will also allow them to avoid relying on strained local power networks.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX, Jeff Bezos’s Blue Origin and Google have also been working to develop data centre satellites powered by solar energy from space.

The satellites would use solar energy to power AI systems that have been launched into orbit before transmitting streams of data down to Earth.

However, Meta’s system would instead beam down solar energy to power data sites on the ground.

Marc Berte, the chief executive of Overview Energy, said: “Space is becoming part of America’s energy infrastructure.

“Our approach to space solar energy enables hyperscalers and technology providers to secure clean power with reliable siting and speed to power.”

Overview Energy is planning to launch a demonstration satellite in 2028 before beginning commercial power generation for Meta from 2030.

Nat Sahlstrom, the vice president of energy at Meta, said: “Space solar technology represents a transformative step forward by leveraging existing terrestrial infrastructure to deliver new, uninterrupted energy from orbit.”

Separately on Monday, regulators in Beijing confirmed they had ordered Meta to unwind a $2bn (£1.5bn) takeover of Manus, a Singaporean AI start-up business originally founded in China.

Manus had been working on developing autonomous AI assistants or agents. Meta announced it was buying Manus in December, just months after it launched its first bots.

Officials in China have grown increasingly wary of home-grown AI technology being scooped up by US giants. Regulators have also ordered AI companies to stop accepting US venture funding without explicit approval, Bloomberg reported last week.

AI labs in China have been scrambling to keep up with Silicon Valley businesses such as OpenAI and Anthropic, which have given the US an edge in the AI race.

data.title: OVO on cusp of being bought by German rival
data.description:
Ovo Energy, one of Britain’s biggest energy suppliers, is on the cusp of being bought by German rival E.ON as the company races to secure its future.

Plans to combine the two Big Six energy providers would form the biggest household supplier in the country by customer numbers, marking the sector’s biggest deal in decades

Sales talks have been accelerating of late and a deal is said to be possible within weeks, though it is not yet certain, according to Sky News. It is unclear if the Ovo Energy name would disappear from the market.

Ovo Energy, which was founded by billionaire Stephen Fitzpatrick in 2009, became one of Britain’s biggest suppliers in 2020 after it acquired SSE’s retail business for £400m.

But it has been plagued by financial problems, reporting a net loss of £135m in 2024. Last year, it warned that a “material uncertainty” hung over its future because it was having difficulty meeting financial resilience targets set by regulator Ofgem.

It has also racked up £17m in regulatory fines in the last decade, including a £2.7m charge earlier this year for failing to pass on government support payments quickly enough.

Ovo Energy has been struggling for months to source a cash injection or sell its retail business to save its future. Last year, David Buttress, its chief executive, stepped down and the firm axed hundreds of jobs in an effort to reduce costs.

Mr Fitzpatrick is also the owner of luxury private members’ club Kensington Roof Gardens, which posted a £26m loss in December.

A deal with E.ON will dash Mr Fitzpatrick’s hopes of regaining control of Ovo after he failed to convince other shareholders of his plans to inject £200m into the business.

A merger between E.ON and Ovo Energy would create a giant supplier with around 9.7 million customer accounts. This means they would surpass Octopus Energy, which has more than eight million, as Britain’s biggest household supplier.

Ashley Kelty, analyst at Panmure Liberum, said: “It would be the biggest deal in at least a generation. There hasn’t been consolidation on that scale for a long time.”

The two companies combined would become the largest provider of domestic electricity, covering 28pc of the UK market.

However, the company would still only be the third largest provider of domestic gas, covering 23pc of the market, after Octopus and British Gas, which cover 27pc and 25pc respectively.

The deal comes as households are about to get hit by a £280 surge in energy bills as the war in Iran drives up oil and gas prices.

The energy price cap, which is the maximum amount energy suppliers can charge per unit of energy set by regulator Ofgem, is expected to rise by 18pc in July, taking annual energy bills for a typical household from £1,641 to £1,929. Ofgem will announce the next price cap level by May 27.

E.ON and Ovo Energy will likely be able to make significant cost savings from the merger, but customers are unlikely to see this translated into cheaper energy bills because the companies’ profit margins are slim, Mr Kelty said.

E.ON has about 5.7 million customers, while Ovo Energy has four million.

It is not yet clear whether E.ON will face competition in its takeover plans. French utility provider EDF Energy and London-listed Telecom-plus had also previously reportedly expressed interest.

However, it is understood that EDF has effectively ruled itself out of the running and has not held talks with Ovo since earlier this year.

Martin Young, founder of utility policy consultancy Aquaicity, warned that many of these households may be unhappy at the prospect of a new overhaul of their energy supplier and Ovo could lose customers as a result.

Mr Young said: “A decent chunk of Ovo’s customers will have been with SSE and might be apprehensive about going through another integration, given that these aren’t necessarily straightforward.

“There is a possibility of customer loss, particularly at this time of energy market volatility, where the summer price cap could be a trigger point for customer switching.”

E.ON and Ovo Energy declined to comment.

data.title: Time has moved on, but our motto is as relevant as ever
data.description:
From the first, in 1855, The Telegraph relied on new technology. That accounted for the telegraph, a “name we trust appropriate, from the sources of our special information”.

The paper was founded during a war with Russia, and a month before its first issue came out, an electric telegraph cable had been laid from the front in the Crimea, linking all the way back to London, which received news at the speed of lightning before it reached the enemy metropolis of St Petersburg.

It meant that on the second day of its existence, The Daily Telegraph could rush out an extra edition with news of the death of Lord Raglan, the British commander in chief.

On April 19, 1858, an engraved device appeared in the paper that still heads our leading articles: a pictorial celebration of the electric telegraph. Above the Thistle and the Rose, standing for the Union of the kingdom, stretched telegraph wires, familiar from those following every railway line in the land. A pair of wings, for speed, held aloft a stylised telegraph dial.

The electric telegraph worked by sending combinations of two signals, made by rapidly turning a handle to the right or the left. Its binary combination resembled Morse code. The single needle that waved so fast was shown on the dial depicted in the paper, around which appeared the mysterious words: WAS · IS · & · WILL · BE.

These words meant that The Telegraph was rooted in history, devoted to current news and committed to future improvement: “We shall be guided by a high tone of independent action; we shall be bound to the fetters of no party; we will be fearlessly independent,” as it declared in its first issue.

Within 20 years, The Daily Telegraph boasted the “largest circulation in the world”. The paper’s optimism for the future encouraged it to demolish the grand marble-halled building in Fleet Street opened by the Prince of Wales in 1882. After only 46 years, it was replaced with an even grander building in Art Deco style with soaring giant pillars of Portland stone and decorative Egyptian elements.

In the centre of the fourth floor jutted out a stunning clock. There had been a clock there before, with the letters of the word Telegraph standing in for nine hours. Workmen with alarming confidence and no safety harnesses dismantled it in 1930 in favour of a huge clock mounted in a multicoloured, modern casing of blue, red, green and gold that outdid the most ambitious cinema design.

The new Telegraph clock stood for modernity and up-to-the-minute news. Fleet Street, running from St Paul’s Cathedral to the Law Courts, could set its activities by it.

Flanking the great clock, on the highest visible storey, two winged masks were carved into the stone representing “The Past” and “The Future”. They were the work of Samuel Rabin, the sculptor responsible for the Modernist carving of the West Wind on Charles Holden’s stupendous London Underground building at 55 Broadway in 1928. In addition to his artistic achievements, Rabin had just won an Olympic medal in wrestling.

One more element distinguished the Telegraph building in Fleet Street: in a carving above the door, a pair of naked messengers, each with a caduceus staff, a winged helmet and winged heels, straining to take the news from London to the world.

After 57 years in this ark of Modernism, beneath which printing presses hummed into the night, The Telegraph moved to the Isle of Dogs. In 1994, as the first British daily on the internet, it began to show that papers did not live by paper alone, but by great good fortune it continued to support its IS with its WAS and its WILL BE.

Christopher Howse is the author of How We Saw It: 150 years of The Daily Telegraph.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Leonard Licht, brilliant stock-picker for pension funds at Warburgs and Mercury Asset Management
data.description:
Leonard Licht, who has died aged 81, was one of London’s most influential but idiosyncratic investors as a senior executive of Mercury Asset Management.

Mercury began life as the investment division of the merchant bank SG Warburg & Co, which Leonard Licht joined as a teenage clerk in 1963. He went on to make a name as a brilliant stock-picker and to be co-leader of the firm’s pension fund team – the most powerful in the City – when Mercury was floated as a separate company in 1987.

Licht’s major clients, for whom he produced outstanding returns over the years, were the pension funds of the BBC and British Rail; the latter, according to a colleague, “thought he walked on water”. Combining encyclopaedic knowledge of British companies with an intuitive sense of value and an intolerance for stockbrokers’ waffle, he bought shares he liked in unusually large portions.

These included significant stakes in the shipping and property group P&O, Burton Menswear, House of Fraser department stores and the chinaware manufacturer Wedgwood, of which Mercury at one time held almost a quarter. When a clutch of privatised regional water companies floated simultaneously on the stock exchange in December 1989, Licht bought 10 per cent of the whole list.

Exercising emotional intelligence rather than dry analysis, Licht won new clients not with slideshows on investment strategy but by telling them simply that he preferred shares “which go up”. His picks did so far more often than they let him down.                

Leonard Samuel Licht was born in London on March 15 1945, the only child of Bernhard Licht and his wife Hilde, née Muller. Bernhard’s family had been furniture manufacturers at Glogau in Lower Silesia (then in Germany, now in Poland), but the couple fled the Nazi regime in 1938 to arrive in England with 10 shillings each. Bernhard was interned on the Isle of Man in 1940 but went on to build a business with his brother Herbert as export agents for textile manufacturers.

Leonard was educated at Christ’s College in Finchley, where by his own account he was a “very idle” pupil. He nevertheless became an avid reader and, “just after I learned to walk”, a collector first of bus tickets of different colours according to the fare and later, encouraged by birthday presents from a grandfather, of stamps.

Leaving school in 1963 with one A-level, Licht wrote on spec to ask for a job at Warburgs – where he had a family introduction – and was taken on as a clerk at £360 a year. His first task for the investment team was to file company reports and Extel cards, the principal source of financial updates in the pre-electronic age, and to man the phones during the short lunchtime permitted under austere Warburg rules.

Though he enjoyed spotting curiosities in the daily data flow, Licht was an awkward employee who twice survived being asked to leave the firm before he found a niche as an assistant fund manager and went on to join the team responsible for Warburgs’ growing pension business.

He operated in a style all his own, commuting to work in a chauffeured Rolls-Royce. Prone to temper and mood swings, his impatience with a Hong Kong tailor’s tape measure caused a consignment of 10 bespoke suits to be dismissed as “a disaster” and passed to his chauffeur, whom they did not fit at all.

If Licht loved and to some extent personified the culture of Warburgs and Mercury, he was neither a company man nor a conventional team player. He particularly disliked committee meetings and the increasingly burdensome bureaucracy of compliance. Though he eventually held the title of vice-chairman, he was best left flying solo.

In the summer of 1992 he took his co-directors by surprise when he abruptly resigned to join Jupiter Tyndall, an up-and-coming fund boutique – led by John Duffield, whose maverick investing style matched his own – in which Licht had bought a small stake on Mercury’s behalf a few months earlier.

Mercury declined to present Licht’s departure as amicable, though personal friendships were restored after a period of coolness. Nor were his three years with Jupiter notably happy; he resigned in 1996 to pursue his own interests, later returning to the fray as chairman of HG, a private equity business that had originated in Mercury before being spun out by Merrill Lynch, which bought Mercury in 1997.

Younger HG colleagues warmly recalled his influenc​e and encouragement, and the enjoyment of outings with him to Borough Market.

Among Licht’s private investments was a stake in Falkland Island Holdings, whose assets included the port of Stanley and its only hotel. After retiring, he told friends he had sold all his shares and returned to his passion for stamps and coins.

He and his wife Judith, née Grossman, whom he married in 1973, were a devoted couple. They lived for some years in Monaco before returning to London, and wintered in Miami Beach. She survives him with their son and daughter.        

Leonard Licht, born March 15 1945, died March 25 2026​

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
