data.title: Virgin Atlantic’s new boss ‘rolls with the punches’ as jet fuel costs soar
data.description:
Corneel Koster has had a baptism of fire, even by the standards of an airline industry well used to lurching from one crisis to the next.

It was only last year that the Dutchman was chosen to run Virgin Atlantic, Sir Richard Branson’s flagship business. Carriers were generating record profits, fuelled by a boom in long-haul leisure travel.

But within two months of Koster starting as chief executive on Jan 1, Donald Trump attacked Iran. Oil prices rose sharply, forcing Virgin Atlantic to add hundreds of pounds to fares.

Today, the crisis facing the airline is so existential that it has been likened to the pandemic, when the company narrowly avoided collapse after scrambling together an 11th-hour rescue package.

For Koster, 54, managing Virgin Atlantic in a time of war has been a leap into the unknown – not least because it is his first chief executive post.

“I was hoping for a much smoother year,” he told The Telegraph during a flight to South Korea. “I was looking at improving our financial results by a really significant chunk. And then this happens.

“We have never seen jet fuel at these levels, with prices more than doubling. The industry cannot absorb increases like this.”

However, he says, “That is aviation.”

“When you work in this sector you know that no matter what happens in the world, it will impact aviation. And since it’s a big globe, things do happen.,” he says.

“You can strategise, execute and plan well but then you also need to roll with the punches that the world throws at you, that’s just a fact of life.”

Already, Koster’s ability to do so has been put to the test.

On taking over at the airline, he inherited a plan for upgrading the Virgin fleet and installing Starlink Wi-Fi that would cost hundreds of millions of pounds but was deemed vital to its ability to compete with arch-rival British Airways (BA).

Within two weeks of the first strikes on Tehran, Koster was expected to make the case for continuing with the investment in an annual board meeting with Sir Richard and Ed Bastian, the notoriously hard-nosed chief of the carrier’s minority owner, Delta Air Lines.

Koster flew out to the meeting on Moskito Island in the British Virgin Islands – close to Sir Richard’s home on Necker Island – in mid-March, as the conflict in the Middle East was escalating.

“We had a strategic meeting about the years ahead but also about the weeks and the months ahead,” Koster says. “The whole day’s focus was Virgin Atlantic.”

The proposals to spend $745m (£552m) raised against the value of the carrier’s Heathrow landing slots – rather than bank it for the rainy days ahead – were approved.

“I got really good vibes in support from both shareholders,” Koster says. “They’re right with us. There was lots of confidence in Virgin Atlantic and lots of confidence in our strategy.

“We will invest a lot of money into new aircraft and new cabins, and that’s where we want the money to be, going into the experience our customers get.”

Yet the investment plan represents a gamble, not only on Virgin being able to ride out the surge in crude oil prices, but also on luxury travel emerging from this latest crisis stronger.

Virgin’s blueprint includes not only the refurbishment of the airline’s Boeing 787-9 jets but their fitting out with additional premium economy and “upper class” business seats.

Ten new Airbus A330neo jets are also due for delivery from this year, again featuring bigger premium cabins along with six private “retreat suites”, Virgin’s most spacious berths yet.

Koster dubs this the “premium-isation” of the Virgin fleet, after which 45pc of seats will be for higher-paying passengers, up from 30pc now. This will be one of the highest levels in the industry. It represents a major bet that the global travel boom, which began after Covid, will continue to go from strength to strength.

That was certainly the case through the cost of living crisis triggered by Russia’s attack on Ukraine, when consumers prioritised spending on so-called experiential travel.

In this latest crisis, Koster says he believes that “demand will be resilient and by reconfiguring our planes, we get the best experience on board and we should improve our financial returns as well”.

“Corporate demand recently has been encouraging but our premium leisure demand has been incredibly strong.”

Today, flights across the Atlantic – which account for four fifths of Virgin’s capacity – are still largely full. Virgin and Delta are offering 28pc of all seats in the UK-US market this summer.

Its services to Asia and Africa, meanwhile, are still being buoyed by disruption at rival Gulf carriers such as Emirates and Qatar Airways.

However, even though Virgin is adding fuel surcharges of £360 per flight to upper class tickets, Koster admits that losses are likely this year.

He says the inflationary hit to the wider economy – including a possible recession – could also have a knock-on effect for bookings.

“The question is how much this is going to impact those people who can afford to treat themselves to flying in the front of the plane,” Koster says. “We need to see what global energy prices will do to inflation and what inflation will do to global demand. Right now demand is very strong but bookings are coming quite late.”

Still, the reality is that Virgin simply cannot afford to take its foot off the gas. With BA generally acknowledged as having made a significant leap forward in the past few years, bosses at Virgin have little choice but to keep pressing.

Sean Doyle, chief executive of Virgin’s arch-rival British Airways, has implemented a £7bn turnaround plan that has cut delays, improved customer service and upgraded its booking technology.

The changes helped propel BA to an operating profit of £2.23bn in 2025, up 9pc. At Virgin, profits shrank by more than two thirds to £74m.

Koster says he is up to the challenge of battling his company’s perennial nemesis while leading Virgin through the oil crisis, having gained more airline experience than most of his predecessors before taking on the top job.

Raised in Haarlem, beneath the flightpath to Amsterdam’s Schiphol Airport, he has occupied a wide variety of roles at KLM, Aeromexico and Delta, managing passenger, cargo and ground handling activities and later taking charge of safety and flight operations.

He also has reams of experience at Virgin, having worked at the airline during two stints which span a decade.

Before becoming chief executive, Koster was Virgin’s chief customer and operations officer – the number two to Shai Weiss, the charismatic Israeli who came to the airline via Sir Richard’s Virgin Media telecoms business.

“I do think that my experience equips me to do this,” Koster says. “I’m Virgin through and through after 10 years but at the same time, I have experience at other airlines. That helps.”

He says that, for now, Virgin’s west-facing network, modern fleet and hedging protection mean it does not need to resort to cutting flights unlike other more global carriers such as Lufthansa. The German airline scrapped 20,000 summer flights last week.

“Strategically, we are less exposed than most,” Koster says. “We had Dubai, which is seasonal and which we ended slightly early, and Riyadh, which unfortunately we had to suspend.

“At times of very expensive fuel, it’s also important that you fly very efficient aircraft. We have a next-generation fleet that averages seven years of age and is very efficient in its fuel-burn.

“We are also reasonably well hedged for more or less the rest of the year. It does start winding down the further you get out but in the near term we’re well protected.”

Koster says that he is seeking productivity improvements from staff and will continue to tweak the timetable as demand fluctuates and holidaymakers and businesses react to prices and the situation in the Middle East.

“We will not take anything away from our customers but we will be more efficient at the core of the airline and that will help our returns – things around putting the right aircraft on the right route, frequencies, some tweaks to our network.”

Koster says he is ready to wield the axe if required. During Covid, he helped implement more than 4,650 job cuts, representing almost half Virgin’s payroll after the airline was refused a government bailout.

“We took the most difficult decisions ever in our careers to ensure we survived,” Koster says. “Shai led the charge but I was involved in those decisions and working to resize the airline.”

Now he is at the helm, Koster says calls with Sir Richard have become constant, with the billionaire keeping tabs on an asset he spent £200m on saving in 2020 as part of a £1.2bn refinancing.

“Ever since I took this job, I have spoken to Richard very regularly,” Koster says. “We probably speak once a week. And he flies us very regularly. He’s really close to the airline.

“At the same time, he has confidence in the leadership team. He checks in but I would say he is also very calm and very balanced.”

Even with events in the Gulf dominating his thoughts, Koster cannot resist turning the conversation to the planned third runway at Heathrow and Virgin’s efforts – with BA – to overturn a funding mechanism he claims rewards the airport for overspending.

He says he has been encouraged by some of the changes recently suggested by the Civil Aviation Authority, including the possibility of a wholesale revamp of fees and the introduction of competition in building and managing terminals.

“It’s really important for us that there’s growth for Heathrow. But that growth must be affordable and the charges need to go towards improving customer experience and operational excellence – and not towards excessive shareholder rewards.”

Regardless, Koster says that decamping to Gatwick, which is slated to get a new runway several years before Heathrow, is not on the agenda.

“Would I love to one day return to Gatwick? For sure,” he says. “Is it in our immediate plans? It is not. We’re very happy with our consolidated operation at Heathrow.”

While Virgin announced last year that it would scrap the in-flight bars that helped make its name, Koster says that, above all else, Virgin must continue to innovate and remain a “challenger brand”.

“We have to lean into that challenger spirit because we’re up against big, established airlines. I think we’re more actually Virgin than ever. We pack a punch and our people still have that spark. We need to be creative and bold, and you’ll see more of that.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Rejoice! The English sports car is coming back
data.description:
Join me, if you will, for a stroll through time to the garage of memories at the end of the garden, where the pungent odour of motor oil mixes with mouldering grass cuttings.

In here you might find your father’s MGB roadster from 1968 – the one in Old English White with chrome bumpers, wire-spoke wheels and the black leather interior that reeked of Rothmans.

The canvas roof always leaked, but it was a joy to be in – even if its restrained good looks and rather limited power made it the sports car equivalent of a Rich Tea Biscuit.

Perhaps, however, the English sports car of yesteryear in your garage is the peerless Austin Healey 3000 from the 1960s – an aesthetic masterpiece, though not if it’s been rolled through a hedge (which is what my father did to his mother’s Healey after a binge at the George and Dragon in Epping).

Or how about the angular 1970s Triumph TR7? My Uncle William had one in burnt gold – which my cousin Andy used to rearrange a lamppost. William had it rebuilt. But you would.

Whichever old English sports car inhabits your own showroom of nostalgia, whether it’s one of the above or a grunty TVR Griffith or a lithe Lotus Esprit – like Roger Moore had in For Yours Eyes Only, or Julia Roberts floored in Pretty Woman – it’s a sad fact that while a few are still on the roads today, the companies that made them have either vanished or changed beyond recognition.

MG was mauled badly before being sold to the Chinese – who moved production to China in 2016. Triumph disappeared into the catastrophe of the demise of British Leyland. Lotus is owned by Geely, another Chinese giant, which announced it was cutting a third of the workforce – 550 jobs – at its HQ and factory in Norfolk last year. TVR exists and there is news of a new model… again.

Last time I heard Morgan, the venerable maker of wooden-framed sports cars from Malvern was thriving, albeit in the hands of a Continental industrial conglomerate, and then there’s Jaguar, which had the F-Type until it ceased production in 2024, though it was hardly the “spiritual successor” of the E-Type – a serious contender for the title of most beautiful sports car of all time. Aston Martin, the modern Bond’s wheels of choice, trundles on but is seemingly mired in commercial woes.

So it’s heartening that in these dark days, where good news is about as rare as a Resident Doctor on duty in England, that lovers of English sports cars have something to celebrate.

Because a new sports car start-up – Longbow – has unveiled a spankingly beautiful all-electric open-top “Speedster” model, which will do 0-60 in 3.5 seconds and allegedly go 275 miles on battery power. While the £84,995-Speedster is a rich-man’s toy – there’s no roof or windscreen – and is available only in a limited 150-model run (most of which are already sold), next year Longbow plans to begin making a Roadster model – a two-seater with a roof that will be priced at £64,000 and do 280 miles.

What’s more, they hope to make 5,000 of them a year with an entirely British supply chain, except for batteries which aren’t yet made here – but when they are, they will be.

If it all transpires, this will create and secure several thousand British jobs in the automotive sector. And it’s just the start: the firm would like to expand into bigger cars.

While I don’t think they have received a single handout from the Government, they do stand to benefit from Rachel Reeves’s economic policies because there are now quite a lot of unemployed automotive workers out there looking for new jobs.

England, says Longbow’s co-founder Mark Tapscott, who is ex-Tesla and BYD, is still the best place in the world to make a light-weight sports car, and what could be better if you’re embracing an electric future where every gram counts? Astonishingly, the Roadster weighs just 995 kilos, so about half of a Tesla Y or, funnily enough, about the same as my dad’s little old MGB.

Which is technologically impressive, and should give us all hope. Because while 5,000 cars a year – assuming they achieve it – is small beer compared with giants like Tesla, it’s a step in the right direction – and one that builds on a great English tradition of lightweight sports cars.

Not just that, it proves that despite the gloom, despite the burden of Labour’s taxes, regulation and energy costs, we can still do it. And, thank Heaven for that, because there’s no better way to explore the English countryside than behind the wheel of an English sports car. It’s what the Sussex Downs, the rolling hills of Hampshire and the Lake District were invented for.

data.title: Least effective Tube strike for years as union loses its grip
data.description:
The RMT union’s Tube strike has been its least effective walkout for years, according to official data.

More than half of scheduled services operated normally across all London Underground (LU) lines on Tuesday, despite the 24-hour walkout.

This is a significant contrast to the union’s last strike in September, when all lines were suspended.

That Tube services were maintained despite the industrial action suggests that, for the first time in many years, the militant RMT has lost its grip.

Thomas Turrell, the Conservatives’ City Hall transport spokesman, hailed the news as a “bitter disappointment to the union barons who sought to paralyse London”.

“Whilst it is a relief that the full damage of this strike did not come to pass, we must not ignore the fact that these strikes come only because the unions think that our weak Labour Mayor will capitulate to them,” he warned.

Around 60 per cent of Tube drivers turned up for work on Tuesday, an LU source confirmed.

Other sources said they could not remember the last time an RMT strike had resulted in more than half of planned services running.

All 10,000 of the RMT’s LU members walked out in September, in contrast to this week’s strikes where only drivers refused to work.

Many had believed that a Tube-wide RMT drivers’ strike could force most trains to be cancelled.

Yet, just under two-thirds of the usual service level operated on the Bakerloo, Victoria and Northern lines during Tuesday’s strike, LU told The Telegraph.

The Jubilee and Metropolitan lines kept up more than half of their normal timetable, even though the latter was part-suspended between Baker Street and Aldgate.

Londoners generally heeded warnings from Transport for London (TfL) that “significant disruption” was expected, with officials recording a 13 per cent drop in the number of contactless cards tapping in on Tuesday compared with the same day last year.

This equates to around half a million fewer travellers, having decreased from 3.9 million tap-ins last year to 3.4 million earlier this week.

Passenger numbers were also down 8 per cent on the Overground and 13 per cent on the DLR. Neither line is affected by strikes, although many use these services to change to the Tube.

Around 4 per cent more people used the Elizabeth Line than usual, however, reflecting the part-suspension of the Central Line across its middle stretch between White City and Liverpool Street. The Elizabeth Line parallels the Central as it passes through the middle of the capital.

Fewer Tube drivers members of RMT

The second of this week’s 24-hour walkouts will end at 12pm on Friday, with transport bosses warning that Tube trains will not restart until 7.30am at the earliest.

Around half of London’s 3,300 Tube drivers are members of the RMT. This figure used to be significantly higher but many have either given up their union membership or joined the rival Aslef union.

The RMT’s strikes this week were called over what it described as the “imposing” of a four-day working week on Tube drivers. Union bosses say this will result in longer shifts of up to eight hours and 45 minutes.

Aslef secured Tube bosses’ agreement to a 35-hour week, which is being offered to all drivers on a voluntary basis. The RMT, however, wants drivers’ working hours cut to 32 per week with no reduction in pay.

Mr Turrell said: “It will be a disappointment to the union barons who sought to paralyse London that their own shortcomings have prevented them bringing the city to a standstill.

“The Conservatives created legislation to ensure this level of service as a legal minimum during strikes, which Labour promptly dropped – and as such, any strike which is more damaging to London than the one we have seen this week will be a blatant political choice by authorities.”

An RMT spokesman said: “Our strike is solid and reflects our growing presence in the driver grade in London Underground.

“TfL need to focus on revising the offer our members have overwhelmingly rejected to avert further disruption in the coming months.”

data.title: It’s not self-checkouts that are normalising theft
data.description:
Do good, honest people ever shoplift? The unlikely figure of Archie Norman, the former Conservative MP and now chairman of Marks and Spencer, seems to think so.

To be fair to Mr Norman, he is not making a general excuse for theft, but rather arguing that the aggravations of self-service checkouts not working properly may on occasion drive even the most morally upstanding individual to pinch a punnet of strawberries.

One might think there is a ready remedy at Mr Norman’s disposal to remove such larcenous temptations from his customers: employ more checkout staff. Yet in 2023, Marks and Spencer said that it had installed 800 self-checkout tills to help achieve its target of £150m in cost savings. In 2024, it even started equipping some of its changing rooms with self-checkouts, presumably putting further temptation in the public’s way.

Given the high cost of employing staff in the UK – made worse by Labour’s increase in employer National Insurance contributions, the rise in the minimum wage and the new onerous obligations contained in the Employment Rights Act – it is no surprise that retailers have sought to find efficiencies. While many customers profoundly dislike self-checkouts – they make the shopping experience less personal and responsive to consumer demands – others consider them more convenient.

This is not a matter for the state to intervene on. What very much is a matter of public concern is the general rise in shoplifting. When brazen theft is all too often seen as having no consequence for the offender, delinquent behaviour is normalised. If there is a rise in usually honest people taking self-service to a new level, is this not the real culprit?
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: GB News accuses Labour MP of spreading populism ‘conspiracy theory’
data.description:
The chairman of GB News has accused a senior Labour MP of peddling a conspiracy theory over claims the channel is a mouthpiece for populism.

Alan McCormick, a partner at GB News co-owner Legatum, has criticised Liam Byrne after he claimed the broadcaster’s investors were “volatility traders” hoping to cash in on chaos in Britain.

Writing in The Telegraph, Mr McCormick said the claim “verges on conspiracy theory”, adding: “It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works.”

Mr Byrne, who chairs the House of Commons’s business and trade committee, has accused GB News’s backers of seeking to promote populist politicians to unleash market chaos they can profit from.

The Labour MP commissioned research for his book, Why Populists are Winning and How to Beat Them, that found more than £170m had been handed to media organisations, politicians and think tanks aligned with the populist Right over the last five years.

In a robust rebuttal, Mr McCormick dismissed claims of political bias at GB News as “nonsense” and insisted the channel provided a platform for a more diverse array of opinions than any of its rivals.

He wrote: “The money invested in GB News by its investors has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case.

“GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligned with any political party.”

In response, Mr Byrne told The Telegraph: “Well, he would say that, wouldn’t he?”

He added: “Ofcom know exactly what they’re supposed to be doing to impose some proper rules at GB News – they need to get on and do it.”

It marks a rare intervention by Mr McCormick, who was appointed as chairman of GB News in 2022 following the abrupt departure of founder Andrew Neil. Dubai-based hedge fund Legatum co-owns the channel alongside the hedge fund tycoon Sir Paul Marshall.

The investors have pumped more than £140m into the loss-making channel since its inception five years ago. The latest accounts for the year to March 2025 show they injected a further £17.7m as losses narrowed to £22m.

GB News has repeatedly surpassed the BBC News channel and Sky News for audience numbers while revenues have grown as the channel shrugged off an advertising boycott that dogged its early years.

But the broadcaster has been embroiled in repeated controversies over impartiality, including its use of politicians such as Nigel Farage and Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg as presenters.

The channel was fined £100,000 by regulator Ofcom in 2024 for breaking impartiality rules in a programme featuring Rishi Sunak, who was the prime minister at the time.

It has also previously been censured for airing conspiracy theories linked to Covid-19 vaccines.

However, GB News scored a major victory over the regulator last year, when the High Court overturned a ruling that the channel had breached the broadcasting code in a programme presented by Sir Jacob.

Ofcom subsequently planned to tighten its rules to block politicians from acting as presenters on any show on TV or radio, but dropped the proposals in the face of fierce opposition from GB News and Rupert Murdoch’s News UK.

The picture Liam Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory

By Alan McCormick, the chairman of GB News

Why would anyone invest in a broadcast news channel today? In our age of streaming and our attention-based economy, combined with declining television consumption, it may not seem like a growth industry. What’s more, the “media mogul” is hardly the most universally loved of stock characters.

Yet that is where Legatum, the private investment firm in which I am a partner, happily finds itself in 2026. Alongside Sir Paul Marshall, we invested in All Perspectives Ltd, the parent company of GB News, in 2021 when the channel launched.

The main reason we invested is very simple. It’s the same reason that investors back almost any project: we believed it would be a sound commercial enterprise. And more, we believed it would meet an audience need that was unmet by the incumbent providers.

Five years later, that judgment has been thoroughly vindicated – GB News is Britain’s number-one news channel.

As of March 2026, it has beaten both the BBC and Sky in seven of the last eight months, both in average audience and audience share.

In February, it had an average audience of 99,000 viewers, up 13pc on the previous month and far surpassing the BBC’s 78,400. Revenues have increased 65pc in the last 12 months, and while the company has been loss-making, we are patient and confident that in time the business will prosper.

Far from making the most of what remains of a declining industry, GB News is at the cutting edge of media technology. GB News is a technology platform, having pioneered hardware that is now being used by the rest of the industry. Visit one of our studios and you will find a slick, automated operation which embraces new tech and AI unencumbered by legacy broadcast hardware and practices.

Why did we believe that GB News would prove to be a sound investment? There is a clue in the name of the channel’s parent company: All Perspectives. To us, it was evident that a major gap existed in the media marketplace for voices that were routinely ignored by legacy broadcasters – not fringe voices, but those of normal communities all across Britain.

The decline of broadcast media cannot be solely blamed on the rise of the internet and streaming. It is also a product of people switching off because the stories which matter to them have been ignored by broadcasters for a very long time. Viewers simply didn’t want what the established channels were selling.

This judgement has been vindicated. Viewers are tuning in to GB News in droves, largely because they finally hear their perspectives being taken seriously and given airtime. And yet they are also exposed to genuine debate, making nonsense of suggestions that the channel is a mouthpiece for a particular political agenda.

GB News guests and panels showcase far more diversity of opinion than its competitors. Rather than the carefully choreographed and contained “discussions” found on other channels, GB News puts those with opposite views into conversation with one another, and allows viewers to decide for themselves.

Some people have their own ideas about why we would make such an investment. In the course of promoting his new book on so-called populism, Liam Byrne MP has suggested that we, investors in GB News, are “volatility traders”, planning to disrupt Britain in order to profit from future chaos by making big bets on the markets.

Investors certainly do plenty of sophisticated, long-term thinking, but the picture Mr Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory. It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works. While there are certainly easier and speedier ways to drive returns, we view this as a very significant, long-term investment in British journalism.

The money invested in GB News has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case. GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligning with any party.

The investors behind GB News have always been guided by the value of competition and free enterprise, which is a fundamental British ideal. That applies to our investment in a media company as much as any other project. We sincerely value an open media landscape, one in which genuinely diverse views compete on a level playing field.

News channels should be free to present their very best broadcasting, to win viewers to their conclusions, and to generate revenue whilst they do so.

Such a liberated media landscape is what many critics of GB News say they want too. If they are as sincere about that as we are, then GB News’ success should be welcomed by its competitors and critics as much as by its rapidly growing audience.

data.title: Channel 4 programme chief quits amid shake-up at broadcaster
data.description:
Channel 4’s veteran head of programmes is to step down amid a leadership overhaul at the youth-focused broadcaster.

Ian Katz, the chief content officer, will leave in the autumn after nearly nine years in the role, making him the longest-serving programme boss in the channel’s history.

Mr Katz, a former BBC Newsnight editor and senior journalist at The Guardian, joined Channel 4 in 2018. He is credited with bringing shows such as Taskmaster to the channel, while he also oversaw the Russell T Davies drama It’s A Sin and documentaries including Russell Brand: In Plain Sight.

The 58-year-old was also responsible for driving Channel 4’s expansion on YouTube as the broadcaster tries to navigate a decline in traditional TV viewing and exodus of young audiences to streaming rivals.

However, Channel 4 has faced criticism in recent years that it has lost its edge and now relies too heavily on old formats such as Gogglebox and The Great British Bake Off. The channel was launched under Margaret Thatcher in 1982 with a remit to make challenging and provocative shows.

Mr Katz’s departure comes a month after Priya Dogra took over as chief executive of Channel 4. Ms Dogra was selected to replace Alex Mahon, who left last year after successfully fending off attempts to privatise the broadcaster.

The former Sky and Warner Bros executive will now seek to shape her new leadership team after a period of uncertainty for Channel 4, which lost both its chairman and chief executive last year.

Sir Ian Cheshire, the previous chairman, stood down at the end of his term last April and was appointed this month as the new chairman of regulator Ofcom.

Jonathan Allan, Channel 4’s former chief operating officer, who was in the running for the top job, also stepped down earlier this year.

Ms Dogra said: “Ian has been an outstanding creative leader for Channel 4 over nearly nine years – the channel’s longest-serving head of programming.

“He has overseen an era of creative renewal, delivering bold and distinctive public service programming with intellectual rigour, good humour and an unmistakably Channel 4 glint in the eye.”

The leadership shake-up comes as Channel 4 seeks to carve out its role in the streaming era. The broadcaster is spending less on making programmes as it grapples with declining advertising revenues from its traditional TV business.

Channel 4 has been striking closer partnerships with its rivals as public service broadcasters seek to build scale to fight back against the likes of Netflix. But it is also contending with a wider reshaping of the TV landscape, with Sky locked in talks to buy ITV’s broadcasting business.

Mr Katz said: “It’s been a privilege beyond words to lead Channel 4’s talented and passionate commissioning team through such a transformative period.

“I’m incredibly proud of what we’ve achieved, from pioneering the evolution of a commercial public service broadcaster into a digital streaming business to backing groundbreaking programmes and talent that have brought a bit of joy to audiences’ lives.

“Above all, I’m proud that Channel 4 has continued to be as disruptive and impertinent as ever, asking the questions and covering the stories that other broadcasters and streamers won’t.”

Mr Katz will remain in his role until October to commission and oversee the channel’s slate of programming for 2026.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: JP Morgan moves roles back to London in Brexit climbdown
data.description:
Wall Street’s biggest bank has moved some of its European staff back to London as part of a post-Brexit climbdown.

JPMorgan is relocating workers from its Paris office after overestimating the number of staff needed on the Continent, six years after shifting services to the French capital following the UK’s exit from the European Union.

The relocation of staff marks JP Morgan’s latest rethink in its European operations, whose chief executive, Jamie Dimon, has been a vocal critic of Brexit.

Mr Dimon warned in 2021 that the bank may eventually move all of its European operations out of London following the decision to leave the EU.

However, it has increased its total number of UK staff from 18,000 to 23,000 over the last decade after launching its consumer banking arm, Chase, in the UK.

Chase now employs 13,000 staff in London.

By contrast, it has more than 1,000 staff working in Paris, a figure that has quadrupled since Brexit but is still dwarfed by its number of London staff.

It is not clear how many staff are moving from Paris to London.

However, a spokesman for the bank told Bloomberg that it was “committed to our sizeable operations on the Continent for the long term”.

Mr Dimon, the bank’s long-serving chief executive, said in the run-up to the 2016 vote that the investment giant could axe up to 4,000 UK jobs if Leave won.

However, it later scaled back these estimates and said no more than 500 to 1,000 roles would move abroad.

A vocal backer of Remain before the referendum, Mr Dimon said that Brexit “cannot possibly be a positive” for the UK economy in the near term.

In his annual letter to shareholders in 2021, Mr Dimon wrote: “Brexit was accomplished, but many issues still need to be negotiated. And in those negotiations, Europe has had, and will continue to have, the upper hand.

“In the short run (ie the next few years), this cannot possibly be a positive for the UK’s GDP – the effect after that will be completely based upon whether the UK has a comprehensive and well-executed strategic plan that is acceptable to Europe.”

However, JP Morgan is preparing to bolster its presence in London by building the tallest skyscraper in Canary Wharf, which will become its new UK headquarters, housing up to 12,000 staff.

Meanwhile, Paris has become more of a financial hub since the Brexit referendum, with major international banks expanding their presence in France.

Goldman Sachs moved into a new, 9,000 sq mt Paris headquarters in 2022, while Barclays announced earlier this year it was moving its European base to the French capital from Dublin.

The Euronext exchange in Paris has taken London’s crown as Europe’s largest stock market, although the City briefly regained the title in 2024.

JP Morgan declined to comment when contacted by The Telegraph.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Why are self-service checkouts so loathsome? They make us feel exploited, researchers find
data.description:
“Unexpected item in the bagging area, an assistant is coming to help you.”

The phrase alone is enough to raise the blood pressure, dredging up memories of fraught supermarket shops.

You find yourself standing at the self-service checkout, basket full, while a queue forms behind you. There’s not a human member of staff in sight and the item just won’t scan.

Now researchers have discovered exactly why interacting with the machines is such a loathsome experience – they make us feel “exploited”.

A study has found shoppers feel that they are performing “unpaid labour”, effectively doing the job of a supermarket worker.

Grappling with poorly functioning machines leaves consumers with a sense of “value extraction”, with customers “compelled to perform tasks traditionally assigned to paid workers, without compensation or meaningful control”, the study found.

The paper, produced by a group of academics in Canada, said shoppers often found the convenience of self-service tills was “outweighed by the demands of complex systems”, with customers struggling to use machines often being left with feelings of “dissatisfaction, frustration and a feeling of being exploited by the system”.

The paper discusses how businesses “benefit by shifting labour responsibility and operational burden on to consumers”, taking on tasks previously assigned to paid employees including scanning, bagging, correcting errors and managing payment.

“Instead of hiring more frontline staff, firms deploy additional self-checkouts, shifting labour to consumers under the illusion of choice and convenience,” the study said.

It added that in some cases, automated systems could also increase the risk of theft.

The chairman of Marks & Spencer told The Telegraph on Thursday that self-checkouts were helping to fuel a rise in shoplifting among “good, honest people” in Britain.

Archie Norman said that self-service checkouts had broken the “human link” between shoppers and retailers, resulting in people simply giving up with malfunctioning machines and walking off with their shopping without paying.

Self-service checkouts have surged in number over the past decade and now exceed manned tills in many supermarkets.

Initially designed for smaller shops of a small basket of items, they now extend to trolley-loads of shopping and stretch down the aisles.

Some brands are going against the trend, however. Booths, the upmarket northern supermarket franchise, opted in 2023 to remove self-service tills from virtually all of its shops to improve customer service.

Mariam Hamam, of Toronto Metropolitan University, who wrote the report, said the study concluded that self-service checkouts were convenient and beneficial for some consumers and were not necessarily good or bad.

“It depends on each shop but our study found that, under certain conditions, especially when there is time pressure, there can be a feeling of it feeling like work,” she said.

“If it does not work for consumers, they feel like they are not benefiting, with the store getting all the value of the exchange and the customer getting nothing in return.”

The study, published in the International Journal of Quality and Service Sciences, simulated automated supermarket checkouts between two groups – one dealing with simple transactions and another dealing with more complex transactions under time pressure. It then measured the emotional responses of those taking part.

It was co-authored by Mathieu Lajante and Dewi Tojib.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain at risk of mobile signal rationing as energy costs soar
data.description:
Mobile network operators have warned they could be forced to ration phone signal access to combat soaring energy prices triggered by the war in Iran.

Telecoms giants including VodafoneThree, Virgin Media O2 and BT-owned EE have issued a stark warning to the Government over energy costs after being excluded from Rachel Reeves’s support scheme.

Some companies are understood to be drawing up contingency plans as they grapple with heightened power costs.

These include rationing access to their networks or slowing down speeds to help reduce energy use. Another option is surge pricing, which would charge customers an additional fee at peak times.

Mobile phone services such as calls and internet data could be the worst affected, although broadband could also be hit.

Bosses warned that rising energy costs could also force the companies to scale back plans to expand their 5G networks, resulting in jobs either being cut or moved offshore.

In response to the threat, a Government spokesman said mobile network operators are required by law to maintain connectivity.

Industry executives are frustrated at Labour’s decision to exclude telecoms companies from a recent support package aimed at shielding businesses from soaring energy costs.

The Chancellor unveiled plans to cut electricity bills for 10,000 manufacturers by up to 25pc under the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS) last week, though the measures will not come into effect until April 2027.

Telecoms companies believe their sector warrants support as an operator of critical national infrastructure.

One source branded it a “serious oversight”, adding that it raised questions about which parts of the economy the Government was prioritising.

Mobile networks are highly energy-intensive, consuming just under 1 terawatt-hour of electricity each year. That is the equivalent of powering 370,000 homes a year.

Operators hedge their energy costs to help weather price spikes.

However, prices have still increased by 70pc in recent years as a result of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and the outbreak of war in the Middle East, which led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial trade artery that transports a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies.

The price of electricity is mostly set by gas, which has risen by 33pc since the start of the war in Iran.

Telecoms bosses argue the sector is disproportionately exposed to the crisis due to the “always on” nature of mobile networks, meaning they are unable to shift demand to cheaper, off-peak times.

Any move to ration or slow down access to mobile signals, which is understood to be a worst-case scenario, would be a major blow to Britons already frustrated by patchy coverage. The UK ranks bottom of the G7 for 5G download speeds.

It could also deal a setback to the UK’s sluggish economy, with connectivity estimated to be worth £6.6bn per year.

Ms Reeves is already facing a backlash from other business leaders over her BICS measures, which they say do not go far enough and would arrive too late to prevent job losses.

A spokesman for VodafoneThree said: “We are disappointed that the Government has chosen not to include the telecoms sector in the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme.

“We urge the Government to consider the impact of rising energy prices on the vital telecoms sector that unlocks growth in all parts of the economy.”

BT and Virgin Media O2 said rising energy prices could hinder long-term investment in the UK, but said they had no plans to ration access to their networks.

A Department for Energy spokesman said: “UK communications providers must legally maintain network availability.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Supercars risk being stalled by Strait of Hormuz blockade
data.description:
The supercar owners of Mayfair and the Cotswolds may not be the first to come to mind as victims of the Gulf War.

But for Britain’s motoring elite, a crisis is brewing right under their bonnets.

Supercars rely on specialist oils designed to withstand the heat and high revs of engines, but Iranian missiles have taken out some of the key Gulf refineries that produce such lubricants.

Experts have warned that a global shortage is looming, risking the confinement of the nation’s Lamborghinis and Ferraris to their garages.

Gabriella Twining, of energy consultancy Argus Media, said three key Gulf refineries that produce the base oils needed for supercar lubricants have been hit by missile strikes and blockades in the Strait of Hormuz.

She said: “Lubricant prices are already soaring as the increases in base oil costs impact finished products. Should supplies run dry, then blenders won’t be able to produce the finished lubricants.

“For now, supercars can push back their oil changes, but it could get to a point where they won’t perform properly without new oil, risking engine damage, so they won’t be able to drive their cars.”

The affected refineries include Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (Adnoc)’s Abu Dhabi refinery, which was hit by an Iranian drone. The refinery produces 500,000 tonnes of base oil a year for high-performance lubricants.

Bahrain’s Bapco Sitra refinery, which produces 400,000 tonnes a year, was also hit by a drone, while Shell’s Pearl Gas-to-Liquid plant sustained missile damage that will take a year to repair.

Supercars used on the road rely on a blend of Group 3 and Group 4 mineral oils, such as polyalphaolefins, which are chemically engineered to remove all larger “waxy” molecules and other impurities.

These laboratory-engineered fluids are essential for the high-revving, high-thermal-stress engines found in supercars and top-end sports cars.

Ms Twining said suppliers were warning that stocks could run out as early as May or June.

She added: “Group 3 oils are now stranded on the closure and now blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

“Alternative feedstock production for polyalphaolefins is also constrained by the closure of ExxonMobil’s Fife plant in February this year.”

A spokesman for the Automobile Association (AA) said: “These lubricants are critical to the sound running of these high-performance cars, and skimping on their maintenance, including the right lubricants, is asking for a very big repair bill.”

Britain is one of the world’s most critical markets for supercars, serving as a global hub for both manufacturing and sales.

Porsche is thought to rely on the UK for around 10pc of its global annual sales, while British drivers are estimated to have bought around 1,200 Ferraris, 900 Lamborghinis and 1,000 Aston Martins last year.
